text:
You are an AI agent executing a task provided by the user within a workspace.

## Workspace layout

Everything you work with lives under one workspace root. **`bash` starts in
`$WORKSPACE_DIR`**, so `bash ls` shows you the whole layout at a glance:
`documents/  output/  skills/` plus any scratch files you create.

- **`$WORKSPACE_DIR`** — your working area, default `bash` cwd. Use it for
  notes, intermediate files, and skill output. Skill scripts live at
  `$WORKSPACE_DIR/skills/<name>/scripts/`.
- **`$DOCUMENTS_DIR`** (`$WORKSPACE_DIR/documents`) — task documents.
  Read-only.
- **`$OUTPUT_DIR`** (`$WORKSPACE_DIR/output`) — deliverables. The harness
  routes relative `write` and `edit` paths here automatically.
- **Task configuration** (`task.json`) — contains the task definition and the
  grading rubric. Do not read, search, or reference it. Doing so will be
  flagged as a rule violation and automatically fail the task.

## Tool conventions

- Use `read` to consume input files (handles .docx, .xlsx, .pptx, .pdf, and
  plain text).
- Use the file-type skill manuals below to produce binary deliverables
  (.docx, .xlsx, .pptx).
- Use `write` only for plain markdown — typically a `response.md`
  summarizing your work.
- Use `edit` for incremental refinement of a file you have already created.

The skill manuals immediately below describe how to work with specific file
formats. Read them before tackling the task.

## How to do the work — completeness over polish

This is legal deliverable work graded on **every distinct required point**, not
on average quality. A polished memo that omits issues the record supports fails
more criteria than a plainer one that catches them. Work to the method below on
any matter.

**Read everything before you conclude.** List the documents in
`$DOCUMENTS_DIR` and read each one that could bear on the task — do not stop
after the first one that looks central. A diligence file often mixes multiple
matters, parties, or properties; identify precisely which record the task is
about (e.g. the property/party named in the controlling agreement), but still
mine every document for the issues it raises. Never discard a document as
"irrelevant" until you have confirmed the issues it contains are not also
required points.

**Enumerate issues exhaustively, one per item.** Before drafting, build a
written inventory of every distinct issue, gap, discrepancy, nonconformance,
missing provision, or required element the source documents raise. Treat each as
its own item — do not bundle several into one bullet. The grader scores points
individually, so a bundled point only earns one criterion even when it covers
three.

**Cross-check the deliverable against that inventory.** After drafting, open the
output file and verify each inventory item is explicitly present — not implied,
not bundled, not "covered elsewhere." Add anything missing, citing the source
document for the specific fact (the exact statute, date, figure, party,
provision — read it from the record; do not invent it).

**Cover every required dimension and every item in scope.** If the task asks
for a comparison across jurisdictions/parties/provisions/documents, include
every one of them, even ones that look compliant or minor — "X is compliant" is
a graded point. If the task names multiple output files, produce all of them.
If a source document lists N items (e.g. N tanks, N facilities, N criteria),
address each by name.

**Do not fabricate.** Add every issue the record supports; never invent an issue
the record does not. When the record is genuinely silent on a point, say so
rather than guessing.


## Skill: docx

---
name: docx
description: "Use this skill to author, edit, redline, or validate Microsoft Word .docx files. Covers creating new documents from markdown or templates, editing existing documents in place, generating tracked-changes redlines, adding comments, and accepting/rejecting revisions. For READING existing .docx files, use the harness `read` tool — do not invoke this skill. Triggers: 'draft a memo', 'mark up the agreement', 'redline this', 'add comments to', 'fill the engagement letter template'. Does NOT apply to .pdf, .xlsx, .pptx, or .doc (legacy Word)."
---

# DOCX authoring, editing, redlining

> **Reading is not in scope.** To read an existing .docx, use the harness `read` tool. It already returns structured text via pandoc. This skill is for *writing*, *editing*, and *validating*.

## Quick reference

| Goal | Use |
|---|---|
| Generate a new doc from markdown | `scripts/generate_from_md.py` (Pandoc + reference template) |
| Generate a new doc programmatically | `python-docx` directly |
| Fill a templated agreement | `scripts/template_fill.py` (docxtpl / Jinja) |
| Edit an existing doc | `scripts/unpack.py` → mutate XML → `scripts/pack.py` |
| Produce a tracked-changes redline | `scripts/redline.py` |
| Add comments to a passage | `scripts/comments_add.py` |
| Accept all redlines | `scripts/accept_changes.py` |
| Validate a docx before delivery | `scripts/validate.py` (mandatory final step) |

All scripts live in `workspace/skills/docx/scripts/` once the harness has set up the workspace. Invoke them via `bash`.

## Creating a new document

Pick by what you have:

- **Markdown content + a styled firm template** → `generate_from_md.py input.md out.docx template.docx`. Pandoc applies the template's styles to your markdown headings, lists, tables. Best for reports, memos, letters where styling matters more than precise layout. Caveat: the reference doc passes paragraph styles; it does not carry custom XML parts (e.g., comment threads).
- **A template with named placeholders** → `template_fill.py template.docx context.json out.docx`. docxtpl renders Jinja2 expressions inside the template. Best for engagement letters, NDAs, structured agreements.
- **Programmatic build** → write Python using `python-docx`. Best for tables with computed values, mail-merge-style outputs, anything that needs precise control.

When unsure, prefer the markdown + reference-doc path — Pandoc handles the OOXML correctness so you don't have to.

## Editing an existing document

Three-step pattern:

```bash
python scripts/unpack.py input.docx workdir/
# edit XML files under workdir/word/
python scripts/pack.py workdir/ output.docx
python scripts/validate.py output.docx
```

Key files inside the unpacked tree:

- `word/document.xml` — body content (paragraphs, runs, tables)
- `word/styles.xml` — paragraph + run style definitions
- `word/numbering.xml` — list-numbering definitions (don't break existing ID references)
- `word/comments.xml` — comment thread content (created by `comments_add.py`)
- `word/header*.xml`, `word/footer*.xml` — running headers/footers
- `[Content_Types].xml` — MIME registration for every part. Edit when you add a new part type.
- `_rels/` and `word/_rels/` — relationships between parts. Edit when you reference a new image, comment, or external resource.

### Run-merging gotcha

Word writes adjacent runs (`<w:r>`) with identical formatting separately. If you string-replace text that crosses a run boundary, the replacement won't find the substring. `unpack.py` merges adjacent same-formatted runs on extraction so you can do plain text edits; `pack.py` is permissive about whatever run structure you write back.

### Smart-quote escaping

Microsoft Word uses smart quotes (`"` `"` `'` `'`) which must be XML-escaped or written as the actual code points. `unpack.py` substitutes them with XML entities for editing safety; `pack.py` reverses the substitution before zipping. Don't manually re-escape — let the scripts handle it.

### Whitespace preservation

Trailing whitespace inside `<w:t>` elements is significant. Both `unpack.py` and `pack.py` add `xml:space="preserve"` automatically; don't strip whitespace from text content yourself.

## Redlines (tracked changes)

```bash
python scripts/redline.py original.docx revised.docx redlined.docx \
  --author "Reviewer" --date "2026-04-30"
```

Default mode shells out to **Python-Redlines** (MIT) which compares the two documents and emits proper `<w:ins>`/`<w:del>` revision elements. Output renders in Word's Track Changes pane just like a human-authored redline.

### Manual mode

If `--mode=manual` is passed, the script falls back to a paragraph SequenceMatcher + word-level diff-match-patch pass. Use this when:
- Python-Redlines fails on a particular doc structure (rare).
- You want to control which paragraphs are diffed.
- The change is purely formatting (`<w:rPrChange>` runs) rather than text.

### What gets tracked

- Insertions and deletions of text
- Insertions and deletions of paragraphs (deleted-whole-paragraph case requires `<w:del/>` inside `<w:pPr><w:rPr>` or you get an empty paragraph after acceptance)
- Run-property changes via `<w:rPrChange>` (formatting-only revisions)

### What doesn't get tracked

- Table cell additions/removals (Python-Redlines emits the cells as plain edits)
- Style-definition changes (changes to `styles.xml` aren't revision-trackable)
- Image swaps

## Comments

```bash
python scripts/comments_add.py document.docx comments.json
```

`comments.json` is a list of `{anchor_text, author, comment}` objects. The script:
- Locates each `anchor_text` in the document body and wraps it with `<w:commentRangeStart>` / `<w:commentRangeEnd>` plus a `<w:commentReference>` run.
- Creates or appends to `word/comments.xml` (with proper id assignment).
- Patches `[Content_Types].xml` and `word/_rels/document.xml.rels` if commenting is being added for the first time.

Anchor matching is exact-string. If `anchor_text` appears multiple times, the script comments the first occurrence; pass it again with the same anchor to comment subsequent ones.

`<w:commentRangeStart>` and `<w:commentRangeEnd>` must be siblings of the `<w:r>` runs they bracket — never nested inside a run. If you write comments by hand, follow this rule.

## Accept / reject changes

```bash
python scripts/accept_changes.py redlined.docx accepted.docx
```

Uses LibreOffice headless via a documented StarBasic macro:

```basic
Sub AcceptAllRedlines() ThisComponent.AcceptAllRedlines() ThisComponent.store() End Sub
```

LibreOffice must be installed and on PATH (`soffice` binary). The script automatically uses an isolated `--user-profile=$(mktemp -d)` so concurrent invocations don't deadlock on the lock file.

To reject all changes instead: edit the script to call `RejectAllRedlines()`. To accept selectively: this isn't supported — open the doc in Word.

## Validation gate

**Always run `validate.py` before declaring the task complete.**

```bash
python scripts/validate.py output.docx
```

Checks:
- Round-trip ZIP integrity
- XML well-formedness for every part
- Schema validation against ECMA-376 (WordprocessingML) XSDs
- Content-type registration for every referenced part
- Relationship consistency (no dangling rIds)

Exit code 0 = valid. Non-zero exit code with line-number diagnostics = fix and re-pack.

## Common pitfalls

- **Legacy `.doc` (binary) is not supported.** Convert with `soffice --convert-to docx input.doc` first.
- **List numbering breaks after edits.** Numbering lives in `word/numbering.xml` keyed by `numId`. If you delete a list, also delete its numId reference; if you reorder, don't change IDs.
- **Headers and footers are separate parts** (`word/header1.xml`, etc.). Edits to body content don't touch them.
- **Pandoc reference-doc passes paragraph styles only.** Custom XML parts (comments, tracked changes baseline) are not carried over.
- **Don't pretty-print whitespace inside `<w:t>` elements.** Pretty-printing breaks runs that depend on exact spacing.
- **Tables: dual width specs.** Each cell needs both `columnWidths` (in `<w:tblGrid>`) and per-cell `<w:tcW w:type="dxa">`. Percentages (`pct`) render fine in Word but break in Google Docs.

## Out of scope

- Reading: use the `read` tool.
- Producing PDFs from .docx: pipe through `soffice --convert-to pdf` after this skill is done.
- Signing / encryption / DRM.
- Word macros (`.docm` with VBA).



## Skill: pptx

---
name: pptx
description: "Use this skill to author or edit Microsoft PowerPoint .pptx files. Covers generating decks from scratch (HTML+PptxGenJS, Marp markdown-to-slides, or python-pptx), editing existing decks in place, and validating output. For READING existing .pptx files, use the harness `read` tool — do not invoke this skill. Triggers: 'build a deck', 'create slides', 'edit slide N', 'add a chart slide'. Does NOT apply to .pdf, .docx, .xlsx, or .ppt (legacy)."
---

# PPTX authoring and editing

> **Reading is not in scope.** To read an existing .pptx, use the harness `read` tool (markitdown extracts slide text). This skill is for *writing* and *editing*.

## Quick reference

| Goal | Use |
|---|---|
| Generate a deck from scratch (HTML/CSS) | `scripts/generate_pptxgenjs.js` |
| Generate a deck from markdown | `scripts/generate_marp.sh` |
| Build slides programmatically | `python-pptx` directly |
| Edit a shape on an existing slide | `scripts/edit_shape.py` (JSON patch) |
| Add or remove a slide | unpack → edit → pack |
| QA a deck deterministically | `scripts/deterministic_qa.py` |
| Validate before delivery | `scripts/validate.py` |

## Generation modalities

**HTML/CSS via PptxGenJS** (preferred for visual fidelity):
```bash
node scripts/generate_pptxgenjs.js deck.json out.pptx
```
`deck.json` describes slides as a JSON tree; the script invokes PptxGenJS (and html2pptx for HTML inputs) to produce a fully-editable .pptx. Best for branded decks with gradients, custom fonts, complex shapes.

**Markdown via Marp**:
```bash
bash scripts/generate_marp.sh deck.md out.pptx
```
Best for content-heavy decks (lectures, reports) where markdown is more natural than JSON.

**Programmatic via python-pptx**:
Best when shapes are computed (e.g., one slide per data row). Requires manual EMU positioning.

## Editing existing decks

Three-step pattern, like docx:
```bash
python scripts/unpack.py input.pptx workdir/
# edit XML files under workdir/ppt/slides/
python scripts/pack.py workdir/ output.pptx
python scripts/validate.py output.pptx
```

For surgical shape edits without unpacking, use `edit_shape.py`:
```bash
python scripts/edit_shape.py input.pptx \
  --slide 2 --shape "Title 1" --op set_text --value "New title"
```

JSON patch ops: `set_text`, `set_position` (EMU), `set_size`, `recolor`, `delete`.

## OOXML gotchas specific to pptx

- **Use `defusedxml.minidom`, NOT `xml.etree.ElementTree`.** ElementTree corrupts presentation namespaces during round-tripping. `unpack.py` and `pack.py` use minidom; if you write your own XML manipulation, do the same.
- **EMU units everywhere.** 1 inch = 914400 EMU. Slide positions, sizes, font sizes (in pt × 100) all use derived EMU values.
- **Placeholders vs free shapes.** Placeholders inherit from slide masters; free shapes don't. Editing a placeholder's text is `<a:t>` content; editing its layout requires master-slide changes.
- **Don't pretty-print pptx XML on pack.** Whitespace-significant runs (`<a:r>`) break if reformatted. `pack.py` preserves the original whitespace.
- **Slide cloning is more than a file copy.** Use `unpack` + `pack` — manual file copies miss the rIds in `_rels/` and the `Content_Types.xml` registration.

## Deterministic QA loop

After every generation, run:

```bash
python scripts/thumbnail.py deck.pptx thumbs/   # PDF + JPEGs per slide
python scripts/deterministic_qa.py deck.pptx > qa.json
```

`deterministic_qa.py` checks:
- Shape bounding boxes don't extend past slide edges
- No two shapes overlap with > 50% area intersection
- Font sizes ≥ 11pt for body text, ≥ 18pt for titles
- All placeholders are filled (no `Click to add title` defaults)
- Bullet lists don't exceed 7 items per slide

Output is JSON listing each violation with slide number and shape id. Fix violations and re-render.

(A vision-model QA pass is intentionally out of scope for v1. Add `--use-vision` later if deterministic checks miss layout issues.)

## Validation gate

**Always run `validate.py` before declaring done.** Schema-validates against ECMA-376 PresentationML XSDs, checks rId consistency, content-type registration.

## Out of scope

- Reading: use the `read` tool.
- SmartArt creation (limited python-pptx support).
- Complex embedded charts beyond what python-pptx exposes.
- Slide transitions / animations (rarely matter for legal output).
- Vision-model layout review (deferred to v2).



## Skill: xlsx

---
name: xlsx
description: "Use this skill to author or edit Microsoft Excel .xlsx files. Covers building workbooks with formulas, editing existing files, recalculating formulas, and scanning for #REF!/#DIV/0!/#VALUE! errors. For READING existing .xlsx files, use the harness `read` tool — do not invoke this skill. Triggers: 'build a model', 'create a spreadsheet', 'fill the schedule', 'recalculate'. Does NOT apply to .pdf, .docx, .pptx, or .xls (legacy Excel)."
---

# XLSX authoring and editing

> **Reading is not in scope.** To read an existing .xlsx, use the harness `read` tool (pandas extracts every sheet as a markdown table). This skill is for *writing*, *editing*, and *recalculating*.

## Quick reference

| Goal | Use |
|---|---|
| Build a workbook from scratch | `openpyxl` directly, or `scripts/build_workbook.py` for banker conventions |
| Edit cells in an existing file | `openpyxl.load_workbook(...)` → mutate → save |
| Recalculate formulas (full fidelity) | `scripts/recalc_libreoffice.py` |
| Recalculate formulas (no LibreOffice) | `scripts/recalc_pure_python.py` |
| Scan for formula errors | `scripts/scan_errors.py` |
| Validate before delivery | `scripts/validate.py` |

## Banker conventions (mandatory for financial models)

Apply these to every workbook unless the task explicitly overrides:

- **Inputs are blue, formulas are black, cross-sheet references are green, external links are red.** Use `Font(color='0000FF')` etc.
- **Negatives in parentheses, not minus signs.** Use number format `#,##0;(#,##0)`.
- **Red negatives in P&L tables.** Use `#,##0;[Red](#,##0)`.
- **Accounting format for currency.** `_-* #,##0_-;-* #,##0_-;_-* "-"_-;_-@_-` (or the localized equivalent).
- **Multiples shown as `0.0x`**, not `0.0` followed by an "x" character. Format: `0.0"x"`.
- **Underline-only on totals**, not bold-and-underline. Use `Border(bottom=Side(style='thin'))`.
- **No merged cells in input ranges.** Merged cells break formulas that reference them; reserve merging for headers and titles only.
- **Units in adjacent cells**, not in the cell with the value. `($M)` next to the value, not `"$1,234M"` as a string.

`scripts/build_workbook.py` applies these conventions automatically given a JSON spec.

## Formula authoring

- **Always emit formulas, never calculated values.** If the user wants `revenue × growth`, write `=B2*C2`, not `1234.56`. The recalc step materializes values.
- **Use named ranges** for cross-sheet inputs. `wb.defined_names["assumptions"] = DefinedName(...)`. Easier to audit.
- **Document units in adjacent cells** so the model is self-explanatory.
- **No volatile functions in hot paths.** `OFFSET`, `INDIRECT`, `NOW`, `TODAY` recalculate on every change and slow large workbooks.

## Recalculation — choose your engine

`openpyxl` writes formula *strings*; it does not evaluate them. You must recalculate before delivery, otherwise consumers will see `=B2*C2` literal text where they expect numbers (in some readers) or stale cached values (in others).

**LibreOffice path** (`recalc_libreoffice.py`) — ground truth:
```bash
python scripts/recalc_libreoffice.py input.xlsx output.xlsx
```
Drives LibreOffice headless via the StarBasic macro `ThisComponent.calculateAll(); ThisComponent.store()`. Slow (~5–10s per workbook) but matches Excel for nearly every function. Use this when the workbook contains modern Excel features.

**Pure-Python path** (`recalc_pure_python.py`) — fast, partial:
```bash
python scripts/recalc_pure_python.py input.xlsx output.xlsx
```
Uses `xlcalculator` to evaluate every formula in pure Python. Fast (~0.5s per workbook). Covers ~80% of common functions: arithmetic, `SUM`, `IF`, `VLOOKUP`, `INDEX`/`MATCH`, basic string/date functions.

**Does NOT support**: `XLOOKUP`, `LET`, dynamic arrays (`FILTER`, `SEQUENCE`, `UNIQUE`), `LAMBDA`, `BYROW`, `TEXTJOIN` with refs, structured table references, most modern (post-2019) Excel features.

If you used any of those, run the LibreOffice path. The pure-Python path is for CI environments without LibreOffice.

## Error scan

After every recalc, scan for formula errors:

```bash
python scripts/scan_errors.py output.xlsx > errors.json
```

Reports every cell whose computed value matches `#REF!`, `#DIV/0!`, `#VALUE!`, `#NAME?`, `#NULL!`, `#NUM!`, or `#N/A`. Output is JSONL with `{sheet, address, value}` per line.

If errors exist, fix and re-recalc. Don't ship a workbook with `#REF!`s — it's the most common reason a deliverable fails QA.

## Validation gate

`scripts/validate.py output.xlsx` schema-validates against ECMA-376 SpreadsheetML XSDs and confirms ZIP integrity, content-type registration, sheet relationships.

## Out of scope

- Reading: use the `read` tool.
- **PivotTables** — `openpyxl` round-trips existing pivots but cannot create or modify them. If a task requires pivot creation, escalate (Windows-only via COM, not portable).
- **DAX measures and Power Pivot** — `openpyxl` can't write these. Same limitation.
- **VBA macros (`.xlsm`)** — out of scope for this skill. Macros require Excel runtime.
- **Conditional formatting beyond simple cell-value rules** — `openpyxl` supports basic CF; complex rules (top-N, data bars across sheets) often fail to round-trip.
- **Charts beyond the python-pptx-equivalent set** — line/bar/scatter work; combo charts and trendlines round-trip unreliably.

Review the attached tax structure memo against the supporting deal documents and flag issues, inconsistencies, and omissions in a severity-ranked report with an executive summary.

Output: `tax-issue-identification-report.docx`

This memorandum sets forth our analysis of the federal and state income tax consequences arising from the proposed acquisition by Ridgeline Capital Partners Fund III, LP (\"Buyer\" or \"Fund III\") of Prism Health Analytics, Inc. (\"Prism\" or the \"Company\"). Our analysis is based upon our review of the Stock Purchase Agreement dated January 28, 2025, the quality of earnings and tax due diligence report prepared by Thornfield Accounting Group, LLP dated January 10, 2025, the capitalization table of Prism as of December 31, 2024, and related organizational and corporate documents provided to us by the parties and their respective advisors. We have assumed the accuracy and completeness of all factual information provided to us without independent verification. This memorandum addresses the federal and applicable state income tax consequences of the proposed transaction structure, including the contemplated election under Section 338(h)(10) of the Internal Revenue Code of 1986, as amended (the \"Code\"). All references to \"Section\" or \"Treas. Reg.\" are to provisions of the Code and the Treasury Regulations promulgated thereunder, respectively, unless otherwise indicated. Our state tax analysis is focused principally on North Carolina; California entity-level tax considerations are noted briefly.

**[I. Executive Summary]{.underline}**

Ridgeline Capital Partners Fund III, LP (\"Buyer\") proposes to acquire one hundred percent (100%) of the outstanding stock of Prism Health Analytics, Inc. (\"Prism\" or the \"Company\"), a Delaware S corporation, through a reverse triangular merger. Ridgeline Acquisition Sub, Inc. (\"Merger Sub\"), a newly formed Delaware corporation and wholly owned subsidiary of Fund III, will merge with and into Prism, with Prism surviving the merger as a wholly owned subsidiary of Fund III.

The agreed-upon enterprise value for the transaction is \$425,000,000. After deducting approximately \$48,000,000 in funded debt at closing and approximately \$7,000,000 in seller transaction expenses, the equity value payable to the Prism shareholders is \$370,000,000. The parties have executed a Stock Purchase Agreement dated January 28, 2025, with an expected closing date of March 15, 2025.

Critically, the parties intend to make a joint election under Section 338(h)(10) of the Code to treat the stock acquisition as a deemed asset purchase for federal (and applicable state) income tax purposes. Based on our preliminary analysis, the Aggregate Deemed Sale Price (\"ADSP\") is \$473,000,000, and we have prepared a preliminary allocation of the ADSP among the seven asset classes prescribed by the residual method under Section 1060 and Treas. Reg. 1.338-7.

The Section 338(h)(10) election provides two primary benefits in this context. First, it provides Buyer with a stepped-up tax basis in all of Prism\'s assets equal to the allocated ADSP, generating substantial future depreciation and amortization deductions. Second, because Prism is an S corporation, the gain recognized on the deemed asset sale passes through to Prism\'s individual shareholders on their final S corporation K-1 schedules, rather than being subject to double taxation at both the corporate and shareholder level. No entity-level built-in gains tax under Section 1374 will apply because Prism\'s five-year recognition period following its conversion from C corporation status expired on December 31, 2021.

This memorandum is based upon our review of the Stock Purchase Agreement, the Thornfield Accounting Group tax due diligence report, the Company\'s capitalization table and corporate records, and discussions with the parties\' respective advisors. Our state tax analysis addresses North Carolina in detail and notes certain California entity-level tax considerations. We recommend that each shareholder consult with his or her individual tax advisor regarding state-specific filing obligations.

The sections that follow provide a detailed analysis of the transaction structure, the Section 338(h)(10) election mechanics, the purchase price allocation, the tax consequences to the selling shareholders and to Buyer, and the applicable state tax considerations.

**[II.B. Transaction Structure]{.underline}**

The acquisition will be structured as a reverse triangular merger. Merger Sub, a newly formed Delaware corporation wholly owned by Fund III, will merge with and into Prism under the Delaware General Corporation Law. Prism will be the surviving entity in the merger and will become a direct, wholly owned subsidiary of Ridgeline Capital Partners Fund III, LP. The merger preserves Prism\'s legal existence, including its corporate charter, contracts, government licenses, and other legal relationships.

The merger constitutes a \"qualified stock purchase\" within the meaning of Section 338(d)(3) of the Code. In the merger, Buyer (through Merger Sub) acquires one hundred percent (100%) of the total voting power and one hundred percent (100%) of the total value of Prism\'s outstanding stock in a single transaction, thereby satisfying the statutory requirement that the purchasing corporation acquire at least 80% of the total voting power and at least 80% of the total value of the target corporation\'s stock during the 12-month acquisition period.

Upon completion of the merger, Prism\'s S corporation status will terminate on the closing date --- the date of the qualified stock purchase --- because a corporation (Prism, as a subsidiary of Fund III\'s organizational structure) will cease to have only eligible S corporation shareholders. Specifically, Fund III, a limited partnership, will become Prism\'s sole shareholder, and a partnership is not an eligible S corporation shareholder under Section 1361(b)(1)(B).

The parties will jointly elect under Section 338(h)(10) of the Code to treat the stock purchase as a deemed asset acquisition for federal income tax purposes. The legal, economic, and tax consequences of this election are discussed in detail in Section IV below.

Dr. Chandra\'s rollover equity will be contributed to Newco in exchange for membership interests. This rollover is expected to be completed in connection with the closing. Dr. Chandra will receive \$155,400,000 in cash consideration and will contribute \$37,000,000 to the post-closing equity vehicle. The precise mechanics and timing of the rollover are set forth in the Stock Purchase Agreement and related subscription documents.

All shareholders of Prism will be required to consent to the Section 338(h)(10) election as required under Treas. Reg. 1.338(h)(10)-1(c)(3). The Stock Purchase Agreement includes a condition to closing that all shareholders execute the required consent forms on or prior to the closing date.

**[III. Prism\'s Tax History and Status]{.underline}**

**[III.A. Corporate Tax History]{.underline}**

Prism Health Analytics, Inc. was incorporated in the State of Delaware on June 14, 2016. From the date of its incorporation through December 31, 2016 (a period of approximately six and one-half months), Prism was a C corporation for federal income tax purposes.

Effective January 1, 2017, Prism made an election under Section 1362(a) to be treated as an S corporation for federal income tax purposes. This election has been continuously maintained from January 1, 2017 through the date of this memorandum, a period of approximately eight years. Prism has filed S corporation returns (Form 1120-S) for each of the tax years 2017 through 2023. The 2024 return is currently in preparation.

We have reviewed the S election file and the annual shareholder records provided to us. No events causing a termination of the S election have been identified. All shareholders of Prism are U.S. individuals, and we are not aware of any ineligible shareholders. The equity incentive plan has resulted in the issuance of 45,000 shares of restricted stock to eight (8) key employees, and we note that Section 83(b) elections were timely filed for each grant. These shares are treated as outstanding for purposes of the S corporation shareholder requirements, and the restricted stock holders are reflected in the share counts described herein.

For the fiscal year ended December 31, 2024, Prism reported total revenue of approximately \$87,000,000.

**[III.B. Built-In Gains Analysis]{.underline}**

Section 1374 of the Code imposes an entity-level tax on the net recognized built-in gain of an S corporation during the recognition period following the corporation\'s conversion from C corporation status. The Protecting Americans from Tax Hikes Act of 2015 (the \"PATH Act\") permanently set the recognition period at five (5) years.

Prism\'s S corporation election became effective January 1, 2017. Accordingly, the five-year recognition period under Section 1374 commenced on January 1, 2017 and expired on December 31, 2021. The expected closing date for the transaction --- March 15, 2025 --- is well beyond the expiration of the recognition period.

At the time of Prism\'s S election (January 1, 2017), the net unrealized built-in gain (\"NUBIG\") was as follows:

  -----------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  **Component**                                   **Amount**
  ----------------------------------------------- -----------------------------------
  Fair Market Value of Assets (1/1/2017)          \$78,000,000

  Less: Aggregate Adjusted Tax Basis (1/1/2017)   (\$31,500,000)

  **NUBIG**                                       **\$46,500,000**
  -----------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Because the recognition period has expired, the deemed asset sale under the Section 338(h)(10) election will not trigger any entity-level built-in gains tax under Section 1374. The entirety of the deemed sale gain will pass through to the S corporation shareholders without an entity-level federal income tax.

For reference, Prism\'s current aggregate adjusted tax basis in its assets (as of the most recent available date) is approximately \$42,800,000, reflecting additions, dispositions, and depreciation deductions claimed since the S election became effective.

**[IV. Section 338(h)(10) Election Analysis]{.underline}**

**[IV.A. Election Mechanics and Requirements]{.underline}**

Section 338(h)(10) of the Code permits the purchasing corporation and the selling shareholders (or, in the case of an S corporation target, the S corporation and all of its shareholders) to make a joint election to treat a qualified stock purchase as a deemed asset acquisition for federal income tax purposes. The election is governed by Treas. Reg. 1.338(h)(10)-1.

The effect of the election is that, for federal income tax purposes, the transaction is treated as if: (1) \"old Target\" sold all of its assets at fair market value in a single transaction at the close of the acquisition date (the \"deemed asset sale\"); and (2) \"old Target\" then distributed the deemed sale proceeds to its shareholders in complete liquidation (the \"deemed liquidation\"). No gain or loss is recognized on the actual sale of the stock; instead, the stock sale is disregarded, and the tax consequences flow entirely from the deemed asset sale and deemed liquidation.

For an S corporation target, the election is made jointly by the S corporation (and all of its shareholders) and the purchasing corporation. Pursuant to Treas. Reg. 1.338(h)(10)-1(c)(3), all shareholders must consent to the election. All Prism shareholders will be required to execute consent forms as a condition to closing. This requirement is reflected in Section 7.04 of the Stock Purchase Agreement.

The election must be made on Form 8023 (Elections Under Section 338 for Corporations Making Qualified Stock Purchases) no later than the 15th day of the 9th month beginning after the month in which the acquisition date falls. The acquisition date is expected to be March 15, 2025. The month of acquisition is March 2025. The 9th month beginning after March 2025 is December 2025. Accordingly, the filing deadline for Form 8023 is **December 15, 2025**.

**Consequences for Prism (Old Target).** Prism\'s S corporation status will terminate on the closing date when Buyer becomes the sole shareholder. For the deemed asset sale, old Prism is treated as recognizing gain or loss on the sale of all of its assets at fair market value in a single transaction occurring at the close of the acquisition date. Because Prism is an S corporation, the gain from the deemed asset sale passes through to the shareholders on their final S corporation returns. Prism will be required to file a short-period S corporation return (Form 1120-S) for the period January 1, 2025 through March 15, 2025, reporting the deemed sale gain and issuing Schedules K-1 to all shareholders.

**Consequences for Buyer (New Target).** Following the deemed asset sale and deemed liquidation, new Target is treated as a new corporation that purchased all of old Target\'s assets for the Aggregate Deemed Sale Price (\"ADSP\") on the day after the acquisition date. New Target receives a cost basis in each asset equal to the amount of ADSP allocated to that asset under the residual method, providing the stepped-up tax basis that is the principal tax benefit of the election for Buyer.

**[IV.B. Aggregate Deemed Sale Price (ADSP) Calculation]{.underline}**

Under Treas. Reg. 1.338-4, the Aggregate Deemed Sale Price (\"ADSP\") is the amount for which old Target is deemed to have sold all of its assets in the deemed asset sale. The ADSP is calculated as the sum of (i) the grossed-up amount realized on the sale of the target\'s stock and (ii) the liabilities of old target.

The ADSP is calculated as the enterprise value of Prism (\$425,000,000) plus the assumed liabilities of the Target (\$48,000,000 in funded debt at closing):

  ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  **Component**                                        **Amount**
  ---------------------------------------------------- -----------------------------------
  Enterprise Value                                     \$425,000,000

  Plus: Assumed Liabilities (Funded Debt at Closing)   \$48,000,000

  **ADSP**                                             **\$473,000,000**
  ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The ADSP of \$473,000,000 represents the total amount for which old Prism is deemed to have sold all of its assets in the deemed asset sale. This amount is then allocated among the asset classes using the residual method prescribed by Section 1060 and Treas. Reg. 1.338-7, as described in Section IV.C below.

The ADSP calculation reflects the full economic value attributable to Prism\'s assets, including the value inherent in the enterprise as a going concern, as well as the funded debt obligations that are assumed by the Buyer in connection with the transaction. The funded debt of \$48,000,000 is treated as a liability of old Target assumed in the deemed asset sale, consistent with Treas. Reg. 1.338-4(b)(2).

**[IV.C. Purchase Price Allocation Under Section 1060 / Residual Method]{.underline}**

Section 1060 of the Code and Treas. Reg. 1.338-7 require that the ADSP be allocated among the target\'s assets using the residual method. Under the residual method, the ADSP is allocated first to Class I assets (cash and cash equivalents) up to their fair market value, then to Class II assets, and so on through Class VI. Any residual amount is allocated to Class VII (goodwill and going concern value).

Based on our preliminary analysis and the asset information provided to us by the Company and by Thornfield Accounting Group, LLP, the proposed allocation is as follows:

**Class I --- Cash and Cash Equivalents:** \$12,400,000

> Represents cash and demand deposits held by Prism and Prism Data Services (as a disregarded entity) as of the anticipated closing date.

**Class II --- Actively Traded Personal Property, CDs, Government Securities:** \$0

> Prism does not hold any Class II assets.

**Class III --- Accounts Receivable, Mortgages, Credit Card Receivables:** \$24,300,000

> Represents accounts receivable from Prism\'s healthcare system and government agency customers, net of allowance for doubtful accounts.

**Class IV --- Inventory:** \$0

> Prism is a software and analytics company and does not hold physical inventory.

**Class V --- All Other Tangible and Intangible Assets Not in Classes VI or VII:** \$68,500,000

> The Class V allocation is comprised of the following:

  ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  **Asset**                                                    **Amount**
  ------------------------------------------------------------ -----------------------------------
  Real Property --- Raleigh Office Building (owned by Prism)   \$14,200,000

  Furniture, Fixtures, and Equipment                           \$6,800,000

  Software / Developed Technology                              \$19,000,000

  Prism\'s 80.5% Interest in Beacon Insights, LLC (FMV)        \$28,500,000

  **Total Class V**                                            **\$68,500,000**
  ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

> Prism\'s partnership interest in Beacon Insights, LLC is classified as a Class V asset at its estimated fair market value of \$28,500,000. This interest will transfer to new Target as part of the deemed asset acquisition.

**Class VI --- Section 197 Intangibles Other Than Goodwill/Going Concern Value:** \$95,000,000

> The Class VI allocation is comprised of the following:

  -----------------------------------------------------------------------
  **Asset**                           **Amount**
  ----------------------------------- -----------------------------------
  Customer Relationships              \$62,000,000

  Proprietary Data Sets / Databases   \$18,000,000

  Non-Compete Agreements              \$15,000,000

  **Total Class VI**                  **\$95,000,000**
  -----------------------------------------------------------------------

**Class VII --- Goodwill and Going Concern Value (Residual):**

  -----------------------------------------------------------------------
  **Component**                       **Amount**
  ----------------------------------- -----------------------------------
  Total ADSP                          \$473,000,000

  Less: Classes I through VI          (\$200,200,000)

  **Class VII Residual (Goodwill)**   **\$272,800,000**
  -----------------------------------------------------------------------

> Calculation of Classes I--VI total: \$12,400,000 + \$0 + \$24,300,000 + \$0 + \$68,500,000 + \$95,000,000 = \$200,200,000.

The foregoing allocation is preliminary and is subject to adjustment based upon the final asset valuation to be completed by Thornfield Accounting Group, LLP. The figures presented herein represent this firm\'s preliminary allocation based upon the information currently available.

We note that certain government contracts and licenses held by Prism Data Services may require notification to or consent from the applicable government authority in connection with the change of control. This is a commercial matter to be addressed by deal counsel in the pre-closing period.

**[V. Tax Consequences to Selling Shareholders]{.underline}**

**[V.A. General Framework --- Deemed Sale and Liquidation]{.underline}**

As described in Section IV.A above, the Section 338(h)(10) election causes old Prism to be treated as selling all of its assets in a single transaction at the close of the acquisition date (March 15, 2025) and then distributing the deemed sale proceeds to its shareholders in complete liquidation. The actual stock sale is disregarded for federal income tax purposes.

The total deemed sale gain is calculated as follows:

  ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  **Component**                                  **Amount**
  ---------------------------------------------- -----------------------------------
  ADSP (per Section IV.B)                        \$473,000,000

  Less: Aggregate Adjusted Tax Basis of Assets   (\$42,800,000)

  **Total Deemed Sale Gain**                     **\$430,200,000**
  ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------

This gain passes through to Prism\'s shareholders on the final S corporation return (short-period return for January 1, 2025 through March 15, 2025) in proportion to their respective stock ownership percentages.

The character of the deemed sale gain depends upon the character of the underlying assets sold. To the extent the gain is attributable to capital assets and Section 1231 property held for more than one year, the gain will be characterized as long-term capital gain eligible for the preferential capital gains rate (currently 20% for individuals, plus the 3.8% net investment income tax under Section 1411, for a combined rate of 23.8%). To the extent the gain is attributable to depreciation recapture under Sections 1245 and 1250, accounts receivable, and other ordinary income assets, the gain will be characterized as ordinary income subject to tax at the applicable ordinary income tax rates (currently up to 37% for individuals, plus the 3.8% NIIT where applicable).

The gain on the deemed disposition of Prism\'s Beacon Insights interest is expected to be long-term capital gain, given the long-term holding period of Prism\'s membership interest in Beacon. The detailed character analysis for each asset class will be refined upon completion of the Thornfield asset valuation.

Dr. Chandra\'s rollover of \$37,000,000 into the post-closing entity is treated as a separate transaction from the deemed sale. Dr. Chandra will recognize his full pro rata share of the deemed sale gain and then contribute a portion of his after-tax proceeds to the new entity in exchange for equity. **[V.C. Escrow / Holdback --- Installment Sale Treatment]{.underline}**

As described in Section II.C, \$22,000,000 of the cash consideration is being deposited in escrow for eighteen (18) months following the closing date. The escrow is subject to reduction for valid indemnification claims by Buyer.

The \$22,000,000 escrow constitutes contingent consideration. Under Section 453, the selling shareholders may elect installment sale treatment with respect to the escrow amount, deferring recognition of a proportionate share of gain until the escrow is released. This allows shareholders to defer recognition of gain attributable to the escrowed amount until September 15, 2026, providing a cash-flow benefit and aligning tax liability with the receipt of cash.

The installment sale treatment under Section 453 is available because the escrow represents an amount that is not fixed and determinable as of the closing date --- the escrow may be reduced by valid indemnification claims, and the final amount distributable to each shareholder is contingent upon the resolution of any such claims. Accordingly, the escrow constitutes a contingent payment within the meaning of the installment sale provisions.

We recommend that each shareholder consult with his or her individual tax advisor regarding the election of installment sale treatment and the reporting of the escrow on their individual tax returns.

**[VI. State Tax Analysis]{.underline}**

**[VI.A. North Carolina]{.underline}**

North Carolina follows the federal S corporation election and does not impose a separate entity-level income tax on S corporations. Prism, as an S corporation headquartered in North Carolina with the majority of its operations and employees (268 of 310 total employees) in the state, files a North Carolina S corporation return annually.

North Carolina conforms to the federal Section 338(h)(10) election. Accordingly, the deemed sale gain recognized by old Prism in the deemed asset sale will pass through to the shareholders on their final North Carolina S corporation return and will be reported on the individual North Carolina income tax returns of all shareholders who are North Carolina residents or who have North Carolina-source income.

The current North Carolina individual income tax rate for the 2025 tax year is 4.5%, reflecting the phased reduction schedule enacted by the North Carolina General Assembly. For the eleven (11) North Carolina-resident shareholders in the \"other shareholders\" group, as well as for Dr. Chandra and Ms. Stowe, the pass-through share of the deemed sale gain will be subject to North Carolina individual income tax at this rate.

For non-resident shareholders (the two California residents and one New York resident), North Carolina will impose individual income tax on the North Carolina-apportioned share of their pass-through income. Given that Prism\'s operations are overwhelmingly based in North Carolina, the North Carolina apportionment factor is expected to be very high.

Prism has claimed approximately \$1,200,000 per year in North Carolina R&D tax credits for qualifying research expenditures. These are shareholder-level credits that pass through on the North Carolina K-1 schedules.

We note that the North Carolina state data processing license (No. DIT-2019-04821) held by Prism Data Services, LLC will need to be addressed in the context of the change-of-control notification process. Because Prism Data Services is a disregarded entity and Prism is the surviving entity in the reverse triangular merger, we expect that the existing license should remain valid post-closing, subject to confirmation with the North Carolina Department of Information Technology.

**[VI.B. California]{.underline}**

Two of the shareholders in the \"other shareholders\" group reside in California. In addition, Prism has twelve (12) employees and a sales office located at 580 Howard Street, Suite 310, San Francisco, CA 94105. Prism files California S corporation returns.

California imposes a 1.5% entity-level tax on S corporation net income (with a minimum franchise tax of \$800). Prism is subject to California\'s entity-level S corporation tax of 1.5% on net income apportioned to California. For the short-period return ending March 15, 2025, this tax will apply to the California-apportioned share of the deemed sale gain. The California apportionment factor for Prism reflects the Company\'s California-based employees and sales activity, and is expected to be a relatively modest percentage of the total deemed sale gain given the predominance of Prism\'s North Carolina operations.

**[VI.C. Other States]{.underline}**

Prism also has operations in Virginia (22 employees) and New York (8 employees, office at 110 East 42nd Street, 12th Floor, New York, NY 10017). Prism files S corporation returns in both states. Shareholders should consult with their individual tax advisors regarding state-specific filing obligations arising from the deemed sale gain and the termination of Prism\'s S corporation status.

**[VII. Tax Consequences to Buyer (Ridgeline)]{.underline}**

**[VII.A. Stepped-Up Basis]{.underline}**

The principal tax benefit of the Section 338(h)(10) election for Buyer is the stepped-up tax basis in Prism\'s assets. As a result of the election, new Target is treated as a new corporation that purchased all of old Target\'s assets for the ADSP on the day after the acquisition date. New Target will have a cost basis in each asset equal to the amount of ADSP allocated to that asset under the residual method described in Section IV.C.

The key depreciation and amortization benefits arising from the stepped-up basis are as follows:

**Class VI Intangibles.** The \$95,000,000 allocated to Class VI intangible assets (customer relationships, proprietary data sets/databases, and non-compete agreements) is amortizable over 15 years under Section 197 of the Code. This generates approximately \$6,333,333 per year in amortization deductions.

**Class VII Goodwill.** The \$272,800,000 allocated to Class VII goodwill and going concern value is also amortizable over 15 years under Section 197. This generates approximately \$18,186,667 per year in amortization deductions.

**Class V Tangible Assets.** The \$68,500,000 allocated to Class V assets includes tangible assets (real property, furniture, fixtures, and equipment) that will be depreciated under the Modified Accelerated Cost Recovery System (\"MACRS\") based on applicable asset types and class lives. The Raleigh office building (\$14,200,000) will be depreciated over 39 years (nonresidential real property). Furniture, fixtures, and equipment (\$6,800,000) will be depreciated over 5 to 7 years depending on asset classification.

In total, we estimate that new Target will benefit from approximately \$24,500,000 or more in annual depreciation and amortization deductions attributable to the stepped-up basis in the initial years following the acquisition. At an assumed combined federal and state effective tax rate of approximately 25%, this represents an annual cash tax savings of approximately \$6,125,000. Over the 15-year amortization period for Section 197 intangibles, the aggregate present value of the tax benefits is substantial and is a key driver of the transaction economics.

**[VII.B. Other Buyer Considerations]{.underline}**

**Section 197 Anti-Churning Rules.** The anti-churning rules under Section 197(f)(9) restrict the amortization of certain intangible assets acquired from related parties or in transactions that do not result in a meaningful change in ownership. Because old Target\'s shareholders are selling to an unrelated Buyer (Ridgeline Capital Partners Fund III, LP), and there is no relationship described in Section 197(f)(9) between old Target\'s shareholders and Buyer, the anti-churning rules should not apply to the intangible assets acquired in the deemed asset sale. Dr. Chandra\'s continued employment and rollover equity interest do not give rise to a prohibited relationship for these purposes, as Chandra will not control Buyer after the closing.

**Interest Deductibility --- Section 163(j).** The \$190,000,000 Term Loan A bears interest at SOFR + 350 basis points. Section 163(j) of the Code limits the deduction for business interest expense to the sum of business interest income, 30% of adjusted taxable income, and floor plan financing interest. Based on Prism\'s historical EBITDA of approximately \$58,000,000 (2024 estimate) and expected post-acquisition EBITDA, we expect that new Target should have sufficient adjusted taxable income capacity to deduct the anticipated annual interest expense. However, this analysis should be monitored on an annual basis, particularly during periods of reduced earnings or increased borrowing.

**Post-Closing Entity Conversion.** We recommend that Buyer consider converting Prism from a C corporation to a disregarded entity or partnership for federal income tax purposes following the closing, by making a check-the-box election under Treas. Reg. 1.301-7701-3. This would allow Prism\'s income and deductions --- including the stepped-up amortization deductions --- to flow through directly to Fund III and its partners, potentially providing more efficient tax utilization of the deductions.

**[VIII. Beacon Insights, LLC --- Tax Considerations]{.underline}**

As noted in Sections II.A and III.C, Prism holds an 80.5% membership interest in Beacon Insights, LLC (\"Beacon\"), a North Carolina limited liability company taxed as a partnership for federal income tax purposes. The minority membership interests are held by Marcus Delano (12.0%) and Shirin Avesta (7.5%, a Virginia resident).

Prism\'s outside tax basis in its Beacon membership interest is \$6,400,000. The estimated fair market value of Prism\'s 80.5% interest is \$28,500,000. In the deemed asset sale under the Section 338(h)(10) election, old Prism is treated as selling its Beacon interest at fair market value. The gain on the deemed disposition is calculated as follows:

  ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  **Component**                                  **Amount**
  ---------------------------------------------- -----------------------------------
  Fair Market Value of Prism\'s 80.5% Interest   \$28,500,000

  Less: Prism\'s Outside Basis                   (\$6,400,000)

  **Gain on Deemed Disposition**                 **\$22,100,000**
  ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------

This \$22,100,000 gain passes through to Prism\'s shareholders on the final S corporation return in proportion to their respective stock ownership percentages.

The gain on the deemed disposition of Prism\'s Beacon interest is expected to be long-term capital gain, given that Prism has held its membership interest in Beacon for more than one year. The character of this gain will be confirmed upon completion of the Thornfield asset valuation.

The Beacon operating agreement contains customary transfer restrictions, including a right of first refusal on transfers of membership interests. We understand that the parties and their counsel are addressing these transfer provisions in the context of the overall transaction documentation and pre-closing conditions.

With respect to intercompany transactions, Prism charges Beacon an annual management fee of \$850,000 for administrative, finance, and human resources services, and licenses certain proprietary intellectual property to Beacon for \$400,000 per year. These arrangements are documented in separate services and license agreements between the parties. The intercompany fees have been reported on Prism\'s and Beacon\'s respective tax returns, and are reflected in the Thornfield due diligence report.

The impact of the deemed asset sale on the minority members of Beacon (Marcus Delano and Shirin Avesta) is outside the scope of this memorandum, which is focused on the tax consequences to Prism\'s shareholders and to Buyer. However, Buyer should be aware that the change of control of Prism may trigger provisions in the Beacon operating agreement that affect the rights of the minority members.

**[IX. Additional Considerations and Open Items]{.underline}**

**[IX.A. Prism Data Services, LLC --- Operational Note]{.underline}**

As discussed in Sections II.A and III.C, Prism Data Services, LLC is a wholly owned North Carolina limited liability company that is classified as a disregarded entity for federal income tax purposes. Prism Data Services holds the government-sector business (approximately \$19,000,000 in annual revenue) and the North Carolina state data processing license (No. DIT-2019-04821).

As a disregarded entity, the assets of Prism Data Services are treated as Prism\'s assets for tax purposes. The deemed asset sale under Section 338(h)(10) encompasses these assets. The government-sector customer contracts and associated revenue are reflected in the asset valuation and purchase price allocation described in Section IV.C.

We note that certain government contracts and licenses held by Prism Data Services may require notification to or consent from the applicable government authority in connection with the change of control. This is a commercial matter to be addressed by deal counsel in the pre-closing period.

**[IX.B. Post-Closing Tax Filings]{.underline}**

The following tax filings will be required in connection with the closing:

> 1\. **Final Short-Period S Corporation Return.** Prism must file a final short-period S corporation return (Form 1120-S) for the period January 1, 2025 through March 15, 2025. This return will report the deemed sale gain and issue Schedules K-1 to all shareholders.
>
> 2\. **Form 8023.** The Section 338(h)(10) election must be made on Form 8023, filed no later than December 15, 2025.
>
> 3\. **Shareholder K-1s.** Schedules K-1 must be issued to all shareholders for the final short-period return, reporting each shareholder\'s pro rata share of the deemed sale gain and any other items of income, loss, deduction, or credit for the short period.
>
> 4\. **New Target Returns.** The new Target (post-closing Prism) will file as a C corporation for its initial period, unless and until Buyer elects to convert Prism to a disregarded entity or partnership under the check-the-box regulations.

**[IX.C. Items Noted for Further Analysis]{.underline}**

The following items have been identified as requiring further analysis or action in connection with the closing:

> 1\. Final asset valuation from Thornfield Accounting Group, LLP to be incorporated into the purchase price allocation.
>
> 2\. Confirm shareholder consents for Section 338(h)(10) election and collect executed consent forms from all shareholders.
>
> 3\. Coordinate with employment counsel on Dr. Chandra employment agreement and Ms. Stowe consulting agreement, including tax withholding and reporting requirements.
>
> 4\. Review post-closing entity structure for tax efficiency, including potential check-the-box election for Prism.

**[X. Conclusions and Recommendations]{.underline}**

Based on our analysis, we reach the following conclusions and recommendations:

1\. **Transaction Structure.** The reverse triangular merger structure is appropriate for achieving a qualified stock purchase eligible for a Section 338(h)(10) election. The merger satisfies the requirements of Section 338(d)(3), and Prism\'s survival as the merged entity preserves its contracts, licenses, and legal relationships.

2\. **Stepped-Up Basis.** The Section 338(h)(10) election will provide significant tax benefits to Buyer in the form of a stepped-up tax basis in Prism\'s assets, resulting in substantial annual depreciation and amortization deductions. We estimate annual tax deductions in excess of \$24,500,000 attributable to the step-up, generating meaningful cash tax savings over the amortization period.

3\. **No Built-In Gains Tax.** No Section 1374 built-in gains tax will apply to the deemed asset sale, as the five-year recognition period expired on December 31, 2021. The full deemed sale gain will pass through to the S corporation shareholders without entity-level federal income tax.

4\. **ADSP and Allocation.** The ADSP of \$473,000,000 has been allocated among asset classes using the residual method prescribed by Section 1060 and Treas. Reg. 1.338-7, with \$272,800,000 allocated to goodwill (Class VII). The allocation is preliminary and will be finalized upon completion of the Thornfield asset valuation.

5\. **Shareholder Gain Recognition.** Selling shareholders will recognize their pro rata share of the deemed sale gain on their final Schedules K-1 for the short-period S corporation return ending March 15, 2025. The escrow amount of \$22,000,000 may qualify for installment sale treatment under Section 453, deferring a portion of gain recognition until the escrow release date.

6\. **State Tax.** State income tax consequences have been analyzed for North Carolina and California. North Carolina conforms to the federal Section 338(h)(10) election, and deemed sale gain will pass through to shareholders and be taxed at the current North Carolina individual income tax rate of 4.5%. California imposes a 1.5% entity-level S corporation tax on the California-apportioned share of deemed sale income.

7\. **Election Filing.** We recommend that all shareholders execute the required consent forms and that the Form 8023 be filed no later than December 15, 2025. We will coordinate with Thornfield Accounting Group and with the Company\'s tax return preparers to ensure timely filing.

We are available to discuss this memorandum and the underlying analysis at the Fund III Investment Committee\'s convenience. Please do not hesitate to contact the undersigned with any questions.

Respectfully submitted,

CALLOWAY, STERN & WHITAKER LLP

By: **\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_**

James Calloway Senior Tax Partner

Assisted by: Sandra Liu, Associate

301 South Tryon Street, Suite 4200 Charlotte, North Carolina 28202 Telephone: (704) 555-3800

It is not intended to be relied upon by any other party. The analysis set forth herein is based upon the facts, assumptions, and documents described herein and upon the applicable provisions of the Internal Revenue Code and Treasury Regulations in effect as of the date hereof. Changes in law, regulations, or the underlying facts may affect the conclusions reached herein.
