**[I. Executive Summary]{.underline}**

Thornfield Accounting Group, LLP (\"Thornfield\") was engaged by Ridgeline Capital Partners, LP (\"Ridgeline\"), through its tax counsel Calloway, Stern & Whitaker LLP (\"CSW\"), to conduct tax due diligence on Prism Health Analytics, Inc. (\"Prism\" or the \"Company\"), a Delaware S corporation (EIN 81-2947365) headquartered at 4200 Lake Boone Trail, Suite 500, Raleigh, NC 27607.

**Scope.** Our engagement encompassed a review of federal and state income tax compliance, verification of the Company\'s S corporation election and ongoing eligibility, analysis of tax attributes and carryforwards, examination of intercompany transactions between Prism and its affiliates, review of equity compensation arrangements, and analysis of subsidiary tax classifications.

**Period Reviewed.** Tax years 2017 through 2024 (S corporation years) and the short C corporation year (June 14, 2016 through December 31, 2016).

**Transaction Context.** Ridgeline Capital Partners Fund III, LP proposes to acquire 100% of the outstanding stock of Prism via a reverse triangular merger with Ridgeline Acquisition Sub, Inc. (\"Merger Sub\"). The parties anticipate making a joint election under Section 338(h)(10) of the Internal Revenue Code. The transaction values the enterprise at \$425,000,000 and equity at \$370,000,000. The expected closing date is March 15, 2025. Linden Rock Advisory, LLC is serving as financial advisor to Prism\'s shareholders. First Atlantic Bank, N.A. is the lending institution.

**Summary of Key Findings.**

1\. **S Corporation Shareholder Eligibility (Critical).** While the S corporation election appears validly filed and accepted, Thornfield was unable to confirm S corporation eligibility for 2 of the 8 restricted stock holders. One holder appears to be a Canadian national on an H-1B visa whose substantial presence test compliance has not been verified for all years; another holder\'s shares may have been transferred to a family LLC, which would be an ineligible shareholder. This is a critical gap because the validity of the S election is a threshold requirement for the proposed Section 338(h)(10) election.

2\. **Suspended R&D Credits (\$3.8 Million).** Prism has \$3,800,000 in unused federal R&D tax credits from its 2016 C corporation year that remain suspended at the corporate level. Their utilization in connection with the proposed transaction is uncertain, and they are at significant risk of permanent expiration.

3\. **Intercompany Transfer Pricing.** Intercompany transactions between Prism and Beacon Insights, LLC --- including a management fee (\$850,000/year) and an IP license fee (\$400,000/year) --- lack formal transfer pricing documentation and appear to be below arm\'s-length pricing. Thornfield estimates cumulative Section 482 exposure of approximately \$6,300,000 over the 2019--2024 period.

4\. **QSBS Eligibility.** Several angel investor shareholders have raised Section 1202 QSBS eligibility questions through Linden Rock Advisory. The interaction between QSBS treatment and the proposed Section 338(h)(10) deemed asset sale framework has not been analyzed.

5\. **Overall Compliance.** The Company\'s overall tax compliance history is satisfactory. No material audit adjustments have been made, no federal or state tax audits are pending, and returns have been filed timely in all jurisdictions.

**Limitations.** This report is based on documents and information provided by Company management and its advisors through January 10, 2025. Thornfield has not independently verified representations made by management. Legal conclusions are deferred to tax counsel.

**[II. Company Overview and Corporate History]{.underline}**

Prism Health Analytics, Inc. was incorporated in the State of Delaware on June 14, 2016, by co-founders Dr. Naveen Chandra and Rachel Stowe. The Company provides predictive analytics software to hospital systems and health insurers, leveraging proprietary machine learning models to optimize patient outcomes and reduce operational costs.

For fiscal year 2024, Prism generated approximately \$87,000,000 in revenue. The Company employs 310 individuals across four states:

> • **North Carolina (Headquarters):** 268 employees --- 4200 Lake Boone Trail, Suite 500, Raleigh, NC 27607
>
> • **Virginia:** 22 employees
>
> • **California (Sales Office):** 12 employees --- 580 Howard Street, Suite 310, San Francisco, CA 94105
>
> • **New York (Sales Office):** 8 employees --- 110 East 42nd Street, 12th Floor, New York, NY 10017

**Subsidiaries and Affiliates.**

Prism owns 100% of **Prism Data Services, LLC**, a North Carolina limited liability company (EIN 86-5512340) treated as a disregarded entity for federal tax purposes. Prism Data Services uses Prism\'s EIN (81-2947365) for federal filing purposes. The LLC holds certain software licenses, employs 22 individuals, and manages customer contracts for Prism\'s government-sector business, which generates annual revenue of approximately \$19,000,000. Prism Data Services holds NC Data Processing License No. DIT-2019-04821.

Prism also owns an 80.5% membership interest in **Beacon Insights, LLC**, a North Carolina limited liability company (EIN 84-3219876) classified as a partnership for federal tax purposes. The remaining 19.5% is held by Marcus Delano (12.0%), who serves as Prism\'s Vice President of Data Engineering, and Shirin Avesta (7.5%), an outside investor and Virginia resident. Beacon generates annual revenue of approximately \$11,000,000 and EBITDA of approximately \$3,200,000. Beacon provides specialized data analytics consulting services to healthcare organizations and relies on IP licensed from Prism.

**[III. S Corporation Election and Status Review]{.underline}**

**[III.A. S Corporation Election History]{.underline}**

Prism operated as a C corporation from its date of incorporation (June 14, 2016) through December 31, 2016 --- a single short tax year of approximately six and one-half months.

The Company filed Form 2553 (Election by a Small Business Corporation) to elect S corporation status effective January 1, 2017. Thornfield reviewed a copy of the filed Form 2553 in the data room along with the corresponding IRS acknowledgment letter (Notice CP261, dated March 2017). Based on our review, the election appears to have been properly filed and accepted by the Internal Revenue Service.

S corporation status has been maintained continuously from January 1, 2017, through the present date. Prism has filed federal Form 1120-S (U.S. Income Tax Return for an S Corporation) for each tax year from 2017 through 2023. The 2024 return has not yet been filed; it is due March 15, 2025.

**Single Class of Stock Requirement.** Under Section 1361(b)(1)(D), an S corporation may have only one class of stock. Thornfield reviewed Prism\'s certificate of incorporation (as amended), bylaws, and shareholder agreements. Prism has authorized and outstanding only a single class of common stock. There are no preferred shares, convertible debt instruments, or other arrangements that could be treated as creating a second class of stock within the meaning of Treasury Regulation Section 1.1361-1(l). The single class of stock requirement appears satisfied.

**[III.B. Shareholder Eligibility --- Findings and Open Items]{.underline}**

Under Section 1361(b)(1), an S corporation may have only eligible shareholders. Eligible shareholders include individuals who are United States citizens or resident aliens, certain trusts described in Section 1361(c)(2), and estates. Partnerships, corporations, limited liability companies (unless disregarded entities owned by eligible individuals), and nonresident aliens are ineligible shareholders.

**Current Shareholder Composition.** As of the date of this report, Prism\'s shareholders are as follows:

  -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  **Shareholder**                          **Ownership**           **Status**
  ---------------------------------------- ----------------------- --------------------------------------------------------
  Dr. Naveen Chandra                       52%                     Individual, U.S. citizen, NC resident --- **Eligible**

  Rachel Stowe                             28%                     Individual, U.S. citizen, NC resident --- **Eligible**

  14 angel investors and early employees   20% (collectively)      Represented as individuals; see analysis below
  -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Of the 14 minority shareholders, 11 are North Carolina residents, 2 are California residents, and 1 is a New York resident. Thornfield reviewed W-9 forms on file for these shareholders and was able to confirm the identities and U.S. individual status for 12 of the 14. The two California-resident shareholders had expired W-9 forms (last dated 2019). We were unable to independently verify the current citizenship or residency status of these two individuals, although Company management represents that they remain U.S. citizens residing in California.

**Critical Finding --- Restricted Stock Holders.** Prism\'s equity incentive plan (adopted 2018) has granted restricted stock to 8 key employees, totaling 45,000 shares representing approximately 2.3% of fully diluted equity. All shares were granted subject to timely-filed Section 83(b) elections, copies of which Thornfield reviewed and confirmed. The restricted stock holders are included within the 20% minority shareholder block described above.

Thornfield was unable to confirm S corporation eligibility for all 8 restricted stock holders. Specifically:

> \(a\) **Six (6) of the 8 restricted stock holders** provided W-9 forms confirming their status as U.S. individuals. No eligibility concern has been identified for these six holders.
>
> \(b\) **One (1) restricted stock holder** --- personnel records in the data room indicate this individual is a Canadian national holding an H-1B nonimmigrant visa. While H-1B visa holders are generally treated as U.S. residents for federal income tax purposes if they satisfy the substantial presence test under Section 7701(b)(3), Thornfield did not obtain confirmation that this individual has met the substantial presence test for each year since the restricted stock was granted. If this individual failed the substantial presence test in any year following the grant, the individual would be treated as a nonresident alien for that year, rendering the individual an ineligible S corporation shareholder and causing a retroactive termination of the S election under Section 1362(d)(2).
>
> \(c\) **One (1) restricted stock holder** --- an annotation in Prism\'s 2021 internal stock ledger references a \"transfer to \[name\] Family Holdings, LLC.\" If this transfer in fact occurred, and if the Family Holdings, LLC is a multi-member limited liability company classified as a partnership (or a single-member LLC owned by an ineligible person), then the LLC would be an ineligible shareholder under Section 1361(b)(1), and the S election would be terminated as of the date of the transfer. Thornfield requested documentation from Company counsel regarding the nature and status of this alleged transfer but has not received a response as of the date of this report.

**Recommendation.** This is a **critical open item**. The validity of Prism\'s S corporation election is a threshold requirement for the proposed Section 338(h)(10) election. If the S election is found to have been inadvertently terminated in any prior year, Prism would have been a C corporation for that year and all subsequent years, with cascading consequences including entity-level federal and state income tax liability for all open years, potential penalties and interest, and the unavailability of the Section 338(h)(10) election.

Buyer\'s counsel should obtain definitive confirmation of the eligibility of every shareholder --- including every restricted stock holder --- for every year since the S election became effective on January 1, 2017. Particular and immediate attention should be directed to (i) obtaining immigration and residency documentation for the H-1B visa holder sufficient to confirm substantial presence test compliance for all relevant years and (ii) obtaining documentation of the alleged stock transfer to a family LLC, including the LLC\'s operating agreement, entity classification, and member composition.

**[III.C. Built-In Gains Tax Analysis]{.underline}**

The S corporation election became effective January 1, 2017. Under Section 1374, if an S corporation was formerly a C corporation, it may be subject to tax on built-in gains recognized during the \"recognition period.\" The Protecting Americans from Tax Hikes (PATH) Act of 2015 permanently set the recognition period at 5 years.

Accordingly, Prism\'s built-in gains recognition period began on January 1, 2017, and ended on December 31, 2021. The expected closing date of March 15, 2025 falls well outside the recognition period.

Thornfield calculated the net unrealized built-in gain (NUBIG) at the time of the S election as follows:

  -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  **Item**                                          **Amount**
  ------------------------------------------------- -----------------------------------
  Fair market value of assets at January 1, 2017    \$78,000,000

  Adjusted tax basis of assets at January 1, 2017   \$31,500,000

  **Net Unrealized Built-In Gain (NUBIG)**          **\$46,500,000**
  -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Thornfield independently verified this calculation against the Company\'s 2017 opening balance sheet and a third-party appraisal obtained at the time of the S election (prepared by Valbridge Property Advisors, dated December 2016).

**Conclusion.** No built-in gains tax under Section 1374 will apply to the deemed asset sale resulting from the Section 338(h)(10) election because the 5-year recognition period has expired.

**[IV. Federal Tax Compliance Review]{.underline}**

**[IV.A. Filing History and Audit Status]{.underline}**

**C Corporation Short Year (June 14, 2016 -- December 31, 2016).** Prism filed Form 1120 (U.S. Corporation Income Tax Return) for the short tax year ending December 31, 2016. The return was filed timely. Taxable income for the period was approximately \$1,200,000. Federal income tax paid was approximately \$408,000. No IRS audit or examination has been conducted for this tax year.

**S Corporation Years (2017--2023).** Prism filed Form 1120-S for each tax year from 2017 through 2023. All returns were filed timely. No IRS audits, examinations, or correspondence audits have been initiated for any S corporation year. No Notices of Deficiency, 30-day letters, or Information Document Requests have been received.

**2024 Tax Year.** The 2024 Form 1120-S has not yet been filed. The filing deadline is March 15, 2025, which coincides with the expected transaction closing date. An extension of time to file will likely be required.

**K-1 Allocations.** Schedule K-1s have been issued to all shareholders each year. Thornfield spot-checked K-1 allocations for tax years 2021 through 2023 and found them consistent with ownership percentages and the terms of the shareholder agreement.

**Accumulated Adjustments Account (AAA).** Thornfield reviewed the Company\'s Schedule M-2 for each S corporation year. The AAA balance as of December 31, 2023 was approximately \$64,200,000. This balance will be relevant for the characterization of deemed distributions in connection with the deemed liquidation under Section 338(h)(10).

**[IV.B. R&D Tax Credits --- Federal]{.underline}**

**C Corporation Year (2016).** Prism claimed \$3,800,000 in federal research and development tax credits under Section 41 on its 2016 Form 1120. The credits were generated primarily from qualified research expenditures related to the Company\'s development of its core predictive analytics platform. Because the credits exceeded the Company\'s regular tax liability for the 2016 short year (\$408,000), the full \$3,800,000 in credits was carried forward as unused.

**S Corporation Years (2017--present).** Upon conversion to S corporation status effective January 1, 2017, these C corporation-era credits became suspended at the corporate level. Under Section 1371(b)(1), a C corporation carryforward that arose in a C corporation year may not be used during a year in which the corporation is an S corporation. The credits may only be utilized during a year in which the corporation reverts to C corporation status, or potentially during the post-termination transition period (PTTP) under Section 1371(e).

Thornfield confirmed that the \$3,800,000 in credits remain on the Company\'s books and have been disclosed on Schedule K of the Form 1120-S each year as suspended credits. They have not been utilized in any S corporation year and have not been passed through to shareholders.

**Key Observation.** The proposed Section 338(h)(10) election will result in a deemed asset sale by Old Target (the S corporation), followed by a deemed liquidation into the acquiring corporation. The deemed liquidation terminates the S election, which could trigger the PTTP. However, the credits can only offset regular C corporation tax or built-in gains tax under Section 1374. As noted in Section III.C above, no built-in gains tax applies because the recognition period has expired.

The question of whether the \$3,800,000 in suspended credits can be utilized against any tax liability arising from the deemed sale or the deemed liquidation --- including any tax triggered during the transition from S corporation to deemed C corporation status --- requires further analysis by tax counsel.

**Thornfield\'s Position.** It is unclear whether the \$3,800,000 in suspended credits will have any utilization opportunity in connection with this transaction. If they cannot be utilized, they will be permanently lost upon the deemed liquidation.

**Recommendation.** Tax counsel should analyze the interaction of Sections 1371(b), 1371(e), 1374, and 338(h)(10) to determine whether any portion of these credits can be salvaged, and whether any structuring modifications could preserve their value.

**[IV.C. R&D Tax Credits --- State (North Carolina)]{.underline}**

Prism has claimed approximately \$1,200,000 per year in North Carolina R&D tax credits. The North Carolina credit is generally computed in a manner similar to the federal credit under Section 41, subject to North Carolina-specific limitations and caps.

No audit or challenge of North Carolina R&D credits has been initiated by the North Carolina Department of Revenue for any year.

Thornfield reviewed the supporting documentation for the 2022 and 2023 North Carolina credit claims. While the documentation appears adequate for filing purposes, the Company relies on internal calculations rather than a formal, third-party R&D credit study. In the event of a state audit, the absence of a contemporaneous credit study could increase the risk of disallowance.

**Recommendation.** A formal Section 41 / North Carolina R&D credit study should be obtained for the most recent tax years to provide defensible supporting documentation in the event of examination.

**[V. State Tax Compliance Review]{.underline}**

**[V.A. State Filing Summary]{.underline}**

Prism files S corporation income tax returns in four states corresponding to its physical presence:

**North Carolina.** Prism files Form CD-401S with the North Carolina Department of Revenue. North Carolina recognizes the federal S corporation election automatically. There is no entity-level income tax imposed on S corporations in North Carolina; all income passes through to shareholders. The corporate income tax rate for C corporations in North Carolina is 2.5% (for tax year 2024).

**Virginia.** Prism files Form 502 with the Virginia Department of Taxation. Virginia recognizes the federal S corporation election automatically. No entity-level income tax is imposed on S corporations.

**California.** Prism files Form 100S with the California Franchise Tax Board. California imposes a 1.5% entity-level tax on S corporation net income, subject to a minimum franchise tax of \$800. Prism has paid this tax annually. For fiscal year 2023, Prism paid approximately \$97,500 in California entity-level S corporation tax (1.5% multiplied by California-apportioned net income).

**New York.** Prism files Form CT-3-S with the New York Department of Taxation and Finance. New York imposes a fixed dollar minimum tax on S corporations based on New York receipts. Prism paid approximately \$4,500 in New York minimum tax for 2023.

**Nexus Analysis.** Prism has physical presence (employees and, in the case of North Carolina, owned real property) in all four states listed above. Thornfield performed a review of potential economic nexus in additional states based on Prism\'s customer base and revenue sourcing. No additional state filing obligations were identified.

No state tax audits are pending or have been completed in any jurisdiction.

**[V.B. State-Level S Corporation Election Validity]{.underline}**

The validity of Prism\'s S corporation election at the state level is as follows:

> • **North Carolina:** Follows the federal S election automatically. No separate state-level election is required. The federal election was effective January 1, 2017, and North Carolina has recognized S corporation treatment since that date.
>
> • **Virginia:** Follows the federal S election automatically. No separate state-level election is required.
>
> • **California:** Follows the federal S election but requires annual filing of Form 100S and payment of the 1.5% entity-level tax. Prism has complied with these requirements in all years.
>
> • **New York:** Requires a separate state-level S corporation election on Form CT-6 filed with the New York Department of Taxation and Finance. Thornfield confirmed that Prism filed Form CT-6 effective for tax year 2019, which corresponds to the year the New York sales office was established and employees were hired in New York. The New York S election has been maintained since 2019.

**Note.** If the federal S corporation election were found to be invalid --- for example, due to the shareholder eligibility concerns described in Section III.B of this report --- the state-level elections would also be invalid. This could result in entity-level C corporation income tax liability in all four states retroactively for the period during which the S election was invalid, plus applicable penalties and interest.

**[VI. Subsidiary and Affiliate Tax Analysis]{.underline}**

**[VI.A. Prism Data Services, LLC --- Disregarded Entity]{.underline}**

Prism Data Services, LLC is a single-member limited liability company formed under the laws of North Carolina and 100% owned by Prism Health Analytics, Inc.

No Form 8832 (Entity Classification Election) was filed with the IRS. This default classification is the correct treatment for Prism Data Services.

For federal tax purposes, all income, deductions, assets, and liabilities of Prism Data Services flow through to Prism and are reported on Prism\'s Form 1120-S. Prism Data Services uses Prism\'s EIN (81-2947365) for federal filing purposes, although a separate EIN (86-5512340) was obtained for state employment tax purposes in North Carolina.

Prism Data Services holds the NC Data Processing License No. DIT-2019-04821, certain software licenses, 22 employees, and government-sector customer contracts generating approximately \$19,000,000 in annual revenue.

**[VI.B. Beacon Insights, LLC --- Partnership]{.underline}**

Beacon Insights, LLC is a North Carolina limited liability company with three members, classified as a partnership for federal tax purposes under the default classification rules (multi-member domestic LLC). Its EIN is 84-3219876.

**Membership Interests:**

Beacon files Form 1065 (U.S. Return of Partnership Income) and issues Schedule K-1s to each of its three members annually. Thornfield reviewed Beacon\'s 2021 through 2023 returns and found no material compliance issues.

Intercompany transactions between Prism and Beacon are discussed in Section VII below.

**[VII. Intercompany Transactions]{.underline}**

**[VII.A. Management Fee --- Prism to Beacon Insights]{.underline}**

Prism charges Beacon Insights an annual management fee of \$850,000 for administrative services, including human resources, finance and accounting support, information technology infrastructure, and shared office space.

The management fee has been charged at a flat rate of \$850,000 since 2019 and has not been adjusted for changes in the scope or volume of services provided. No formal management services agreement exists between Prism and Beacon. The fee is referenced in Section 6.4 of the Beacon Insights LLC Operating Agreement, which provides that administrative fees shall be paid to Prism \"in such amounts as determined by the Managing Member.\" No independent approval or third-party benchmarking is referenced or required.

No transfer pricing study or benchmarking analysis has been performed to establish that the \$850,000 fee reflects arm\'s-length consideration for the services rendered.

**Thornfield Observation.** Based on our review of Prism\'s internal cost allocation methodology, including headcount allocation, direct expense analysis, and overhead absorption rates, the actual cost of services provided by Prism to Beacon appears to be approximately \$1,100,000 to \$1,250,000 per year. The current \$850,000 charge is therefore likely below arm\'s-length pricing, resulting in an understatement of Beacon\'s deductible expenses and a corresponding understatement of Prism\'s service fee income.

**Risk Assessment.** Under Section 482 of the Internal Revenue Code, the IRS has authority to reallocate income between related parties to clearly reflect income. If the IRS determined that the management fee should have been \$1,200,000 (the midpoint of Thornfield\'s estimate), the annual undercharge would be approximately \$350,000, resulting in additional pass-through income to Prism\'s shareholders of \$350,000 per year. Over the period during which the flat fee has been in effect (2019 through 2024, six tax years), the cumulative understatement could be approximately \$2,100,000.

**Recommendation.** A formal transfer pricing study should be commissioned, and Prism should consider a true-up adjustment or contemporaneous documentation before closing. Consideration should also be given to whether amended returns for prior years are warranted.

**[VII.B. IP License Fee --- Prism to Beacon Insights]{.underline}**

Prism licenses certain proprietary software and data analytics intellectual property to Beacon Insights for an annual fee of \$400,000. The license fee has been in place at this level since Beacon\'s formation and has not been adjusted.

No formal IP license agreement exists between Prism and Beacon beyond a reference in Section 6.5 of the Beacon Insights LLC Operating Agreement, which authorizes Prism to charge \"a reasonable license fee\" for use of Prism\'s proprietary technology. No transfer pricing study or benchmarking analysis has been performed.

**Thornfield Observation.** The IP licensed to Beacon constitutes a core element of Beacon\'s service delivery platform and revenue generation capability. Given that Beacon generates approximately \$11,000,000 in annual revenue using this licensed IP, the \$400,000 annual fee represents approximately 3.6% of Beacon\'s revenue. Industry benchmarks for comparable software and IP licenses in the healthcare analytics space typically range from 8% to 15% of licensee revenue. The \$400,000 fee appears to be significantly below arm\'s-length pricing.

If the IRS were to adjust the license fee to, for example, 10% of Beacon\'s revenue (\$1,100,000), the annual undercharge would be approximately \$700,000, resulting in additional pass-through income to Prism\'s shareholders and correspondingly reduced income allocable to Beacon\'s members.

Over the six-year period from 2019 through 2024, the cumulative exposure from the IP license fee alone is approximately \$4,200,000.

**Combined Section 482 Exposure.** Taking the management fee and IP license fee together, the combined cumulative Section 482 exposure for the 2019--2024 period is approximately **\$6,300,000**.

**Critical Observation.** Because Prism is an S corporation and Beacon is a partnership, any reallocation of income under Section 482 would flow through to different sets of individuals in different proportions. Prism\'s income flows to its shareholders (Chandra 52%, Stowe 28%, minority holders 20%). Beacon\'s income flows 80.5% to Prism (and then through to Prism\'s shareholders) and 19.5% to Delano and Avesta directly. A reallocation of income from Beacon to Prism would increase the income reportable by all Prism shareholders --- including those who have no direct interest in Beacon --- while decreasing the income reportable by Delano (12.0%) and Avesta (7.5%) on their direct Beacon interests. The cross-entity impact of any Section 482 adjustment would thus affect the economic positions of multiple parties to the transaction.

**Recommendation.** Formal transfer pricing documentation should be prepared, and intercompany pricing should be adjusted to arm\'s-length levels prospectively. Consideration should be given to whether a voluntary disclosure or amended returns are warranted for prior years. The potential Section 482 exposure should be reflected in the transaction\'s risk allocation and indemnification provisions.

**[VIII. Equity Compensation and Shareholder Analysis]{.underline}**

**[VIII.A. Equity Incentive Plan --- Restricted Stock]{.underline}**

Prism adopted an equity incentive plan in 2018 authorizing the grant of restricted stock to key employees. A total of 45,000 shares of restricted common stock have been granted to 8 key employees, representing approximately 2.3% of fully diluted equity.

All restricted stock grants were made subject to Section 83(b) elections, requiring the recipients to include the fair market value of the stock (less any amount paid) in gross income in the year of the grant, notwithstanding that the shares were subject to a substantial risk of forfeiture. Thornfield reviewed copies of the Section 83(b) election forms for all 8 recipients. All elections appear to have been timely filed within 30 days of the respective grant dates.

Grant dates range from March 2018 through November 2022. The earliest grants will have been held for approximately 7 years as of the anticipated closing date.

Under the terms of the equity incentive plan, all unvested restricted stock accelerates and becomes fully vested upon a change of control, as defined in the plan. The proposed merger constitutes a change of control.

The 8 restricted stock holders are included within the minority shareholder group that collectively holds approximately 20% of Prism\'s equity.

**Cross-Reference to Section III.B.** As noted in Section III.B above, Thornfield was unable to confirm S corporation shareholder eligibility for 2 of the 8 restricted stock holders. This remains a critical open item that must be resolved before closing.

**[VIII.B. QSBS --- Section 1202 Considerations]{.underline}**

During the course of due diligence, Thornfield was informed by Linden Rock Advisory, LLC (financial advisor to Prism\'s shareholders) that several of the angel investor shareholders have inquired about the potential applicability of the Section 1202 qualified small business stock (QSBS) exclusion to their shares of Prism stock.

**Background.** Prism was a C corporation from June 14, 2016, through December 31, 2016 --- a period of approximately six and one-half months. The Company elected S corporation status effective January 1, 2017.

Section 1202 provides an exclusion from gain on the sale or exchange of \"qualified small business stock.\" For stock acquired after September 27, 2010, the exclusion may be up to 100% of the gain, subject to per-issuer and per-taxpayer limitations. To qualify for QSBS treatment, among other requirements:

> \(a\) the stock must have been issued by a C corporation at the time of issuance;
>
> \(b\) the issuing corporation must have been a \"qualified small business\" with aggregate gross assets not exceeding \$50,000,000 at all times before and immediately after the issuance;
>
> \(c\) the stock must have been acquired by the taxpayer at original issuance in exchange for money, property, or services; and
>
> \(d\) the taxpayer must have held the stock for more than 5 years.

**Thornfield\'s Observations.**

*Shares Issued During the C Corporation Period.* Stock issued during the C corporation period (June 14, 2016 through December 31, 2016) may qualify as QSBS if all requirements of Section 1202 were met at the time of issuance. Prism\'s gross assets at formation and through December 31, 2016, were well below \$50,000,000 (total assets as of December 31, 2016: approximately \$4,200,000).

*Shares Issued During the S Corporation Period.* Stock issued after January 1, 2017 (during the S corporation period) is generally not eligible for QSBS treatment because Section 1202(c)(1) requires that the stock be issued by a \"C corporation.\" S corporations are expressly excluded from the definition of a qualified small business under Section 1202(d)(1).

*Capitalization Table Analysis.* Based on the capitalization table provided by Company counsel:

> • Dr. Naveen Chandra and Rachel Stowe received their founder shares at incorporation on June 14, 2016 (during the C corporation period).
>
> • Four (4) of the 14 angel investors acquired shares in a seed financing round that closed on November 15, 2016 (during the C corporation period).
>
> • The remaining ten (10) angel investors and all eight (8) restricted stock holders acquired their shares after January 1, 2017, during the S corporation period.

Therefore, only the founder shares (Chandra and Stowe) and the shares held by the 4 seed-round angel investors were potentially issued during the C corporation period and could meet the C corporation issuance requirement of Section 1202.

**Critical Question --- Interaction with Section 338(h)(10).** The proposed Section 338(h)(10) election would treat the transaction as a deemed asset sale by the S corporation, followed by a deemed liquidation --- not as a sale of stock by individual shareholders. Section 1202 by its terms applies to \"gain from the sale or exchange of qualified small business stock.\" If the transaction is recharacterized as a deemed asset sale rather than a stock sale for federal tax purposes, the QSBS exclusion may not be available because the shareholders are not treated as selling their stock.

**Additional Complication.** Even for shares issued during the C corporation period, while the 5-year holding period requirement appears satisfied (shares issued in 2016, transaction closing in 2025, representing more than 8 years of holding), the interaction of the QSBS rules with S corporation status and the Section 338(h)(10) deemed sale framework has not been analyzed. It is unclear whether the intervening S corporation years affect the QSBS eligibility of shares originally issued during the C corporation period.

**Recommendation.** Tax counsel should provide a definitive analysis of QSBS eligibility for those shareholders whose shares were issued during the C corporation period, with particular attention to the interaction between Section 1202 and the Section 338(h)(10) election. The angel investors who believe they may hold QSBS need to understand the impact of the deemed asset sale treatment on their potential exclusion. If the Section 338(h)(10) election forecloses QSBS treatment, this may be a material consideration in the election decision for affected shareholders.

**[IX. Tax Attributes and Carryforwards]{.underline}**

**[IX.A. Federal R&D Credits --- Suspended C Corporation Credits]{.underline}**

As detailed in Section IV.B, Prism has \$3,800,000 in unused federal R&D tax credits generated during its 2016 C corporation short year. These credits have a 20-year carryforward period under Section 39(a), meaning they would expire after the 2036 tax year if not utilized.

The credits are currently suspended at the corporate level because Prism is an S corporation. Under Section 1371(b)(1), they may only be utilized against C corporation tax liability.

Thornfield analyzed three potential utilization scenarios in connection with the proposed transaction:

> **Scenario 1 --- Built-In Gains Tax Offset.** Under Section 1374(b)(3), business credit carryforwards from C corporation years may be applied to offset the built-in gains tax. However, as concluded in Section III.C above, the 5-year BIG recognition period expired on December 31, 2021, and no BIG tax applies to the deemed asset sale. The credits cannot be utilized under this scenario.
>
> **Scenario 2 --- Post-Termination Transition Period (PTTP).** Under Section 1371(e), during the PTTP following termination of an S election, certain items may be deducted by shareholders. However, Section 1371(e) expressly references \"deductions\" and \"losses,\" and the applicability of the PTTP mechanism to suspended credits (as opposed to deduction carryforwards) is not clearly established. This scenario requires further analysis by tax counsel.
>
> **Scenario 3 --- Deemed Liquidation and Final Return.** The Section 338(h)(10) election results in a deemed liquidation of the old S corporation target. Whether this deemed liquidation triggers a final C corporation return --- during which the suspended credits could theoretically be applied --- or merely completes the S corporation\'s final tax year is a question of law that Thornfield defers to tax counsel.

**Conclusion.** The \$3,800,000 in suspended R&D credits appear to be at significant risk of permanent expiration in connection with this transaction. Tax counsel should analyze whether any structuring alternatives could preserve these credits and, if preservation is not possible, the appropriate treatment of the credits on the final return.

**[IX.B. Net Operating Loss Carryforwards]{.underline}**

Prism has no federal net operating loss carryforwards. The Company has been profitable in all tax years since 2018.

Prism had a minor state NOL in California from tax year 2017 (approximately \$180,000), which was fully utilized against California-apportioned income in subsequent years. No California NOL carryforwards remain.

The AAA balance of \$64,200,000 appears sufficient to absorb a substantial portion of the deemed liquidating distribution, but this should be confirmed by tax counsel after the deemed sale gain computation is finalized and the impact of the final-year income allocation on AAA is determined.

**[X. Other Tax Matters]{.underline}**

**[X.A. Sales and Use Tax]{.underline}**

Prism\'s primary revenue stream is derived from software licensing under a Software-as-a-Service (SaaS) model. The taxability of SaaS for sales and use tax purposes varies by state:

> • **North Carolina:** SaaS is generally not subject to sales tax and is treated as a nontaxable service.
>
> • **California:** SaaS is generally exempt from sales tax under current guidance.
>
> • **Virginia:** SaaS is generally exempt from sales tax.
>
> • **New York:** SaaS may be subject to sales tax as a taxable sale of pre-written computer software. Thornfield noted that Prism has not been collecting New York sales tax on its SaaS subscription revenue sourced to New York customers. Based on Prism\'s New York-sourced revenue, the potential exposure is estimated at \$150,000 to \$300,000, inclusive of potential penalties and interest.

**Recommendation.** The New York sales tax exposure should be addressed, potentially through a voluntary disclosure agreement with the New York Department of Taxation and Finance, which may provide penalty abatement.

Prism has filed all federal employment tax returns (Forms 941, 940) timely for all periods reviewed. State income tax withholding and unemployment insurance tax returns have been filed in North Carolina, Virginia, California, and New York. No material issues were identified.

**Shareholder-Employee Compensation.** Dr. Naveen Chandra receives an annual salary of \$475,000; Rachel Stowe receives an annual salary of \$425,000. These compensation levels appear reasonable and consistent with industry benchmarks for similarly situated executives at healthcare technology companies of comparable size. Thornfield does not believe there is a meaningful risk of IRS recharacterization of distributions as compensation under the Sections 1366 and 1368 framework for S corporation officer compensation.

Prism owns the office building located at 4200 Lake Boone Trail, Suite 500, Raleigh, NC 27607. The current assessed value for property tax purposes is approximately \$12,800,000. Property taxes are current with no delinquencies or pending appeals.

All other locations (Virginia, California, and New York offices) are leased. No property tax issues were identified with respect to leased locations.

  7                 Two California-resident shareholders have expired W-9 forms (last dated 2019)                                                                                                                                                                  **Low**        Administrative; potential S corporation eligibility concern if citizenship or residency status has changed                        Obtain updated W-9 forms and confirm current status
  -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Thornfield recommends that all Critical and Moderate findings be addressed before or as a condition to closing. Low-severity items should be addressed promptly but are not expected to impede the transaction.

**[XII. Scope and Limitations]{.underline}**

This Tax Due Diligence Report was prepared by Thornfield Accounting Group, LLP at the request of Ridgeline Capital Partners, LP and Calloway, Stern & Whitaker LLP in connection with the proposed acquisition of Prism Health Analytics, Inc. (Project Prism).

Our review was based on documents provided through the virtual data room (Rooms 7.1 through 7.14 --- Tax), management interviews conducted on December 2--3, 2024, at the Company\'s headquarters in Raleigh, North Carolina, and supplemental information received through January 10, 2025.

Thornfield did not perform an audit, review, or compilation of Prism\'s financial statements in connection with this engagement. Our procedures were limited to tax due diligence and do not constitute an opinion on the Company\'s financial condition.

We did not provide legal opinions on any tax matter addressed in this report. Legal conclusions --- including the validity of the S corporation election, the mechanics and consequences of the Section 338(h)(10) election, the applicability of Section 1202 (QSBS), and the interpretation of Section 482 (transfer pricing) --- are deferred to tax counsel for definitive analysis.

Our findings are subject to the completeness and accuracy of the information provided to us by Prism management, Company counsel, and their advisors. Thornfield has not independently verified all representations made by management.

This report is intended solely for the use of Ridgeline Capital Partners, LP, Ridgeline Capital Partners Fund III, LP, Ridgeline Capital GP III, LLC, and Calloway, Stern & Whitaker LLP and should not be distributed to or relied upon by any other party without the prior written consent of Thornfield Accounting Group, LLP.

**[Appendix B --- Glossary of Tax Terms]{.underline}**

**S Corporation.** A corporation that has elected to pass its income, losses, deductions, and credits through to its shareholders for federal income tax purposes under Subchapter S of the Internal Revenue Code (Sections 1361--1379).

**Section 338(h)(10).** A provision of the Internal Revenue Code that allows the buyer and seller to jointly elect to treat a stock purchase as a deemed asset purchase for federal tax purposes. The target corporation is treated as having sold all of its assets at fair market value, followed by a deemed liquidation.

**ADSP (Aggregate Deemed Sale Price).** The total price at which the old target corporation is treated as having sold all of its assets in a Section 338(h)(10) transaction.

**BIG Tax (Built-In Gains Tax).** Under Section 1374, a corporate-level tax imposed on S corporations that were formerly C corporations if they recognize built-in gains during the 5-year recognition period following the S election.

**Section 1374.** The Internal Revenue Code section imposing the built-in gains tax on S corporations that converted from C corporation status.

**QSBS / Section 1202.** Qualified Small Business Stock. Section 1202 provides an exclusion from federal capital gains tax (up to 100% for stock acquired after September 27, 2010) on the sale of stock in a qualified small business, subject to holding period and other requirements.

**Section 482.** The Internal Revenue Code section authorizing the IRS to reallocate income, deductions, and credits between commonly controlled entities to clearly reflect income and prevent tax avoidance through non-arm\'s-length pricing.

**Section 83(b) Election.** An election under Section 83(b) allowing a service provider who receives property subject to a substantial risk of forfeiture to include the property\'s fair market value in gross income in the year of receipt, rather than deferring until the risk of forfeiture lapses.

**AAA (Accumulated Adjustments Account).** An S corporation tax attribute that tracks the cumulative net income (reduced by distributions and losses) for S corporation years. AAA is used to characterize distributions as tax-free returns of previously taxed income.

**PTTP (Post-Termination Transition Period).** The period (generally one year) following the termination of an S corporation election during which certain S corporation tax attributes may be utilized.

**Disregarded Entity.** An entity that is not treated as separate from its owner for federal income tax purposes, such as a single-member LLC that has not elected to be classified as a corporation.

**Hot Assets / Section 751.** Under Section 751, certain partnership assets --- including unrealized receivables and substantially appreciated inventory items --- are treated as \"hot assets\" and may trigger ordinary income (rather than capital gain) upon the sale or exchange of a partnership interest.

*--- End of Report ---*

**[ARTICLE II --- THE MERGER; CONSIDERATION]{.underline}**

**Section 2.1 --- The Merger**

At the Effective Time, and subject to and upon the terms and conditions of this Agreement and the applicable provisions of the Delaware General Corporation Law (the \"DGCL\"), Merger Sub shall be merged with and into the Company (the \"Merger\"). Following the Merger, the separate corporate existence of Merger Sub shall thereupon cease, and the Company shall continue as the surviving corporation in the Merger (the \"Surviving Corporation\"). The Surviving Corporation shall be a direct, wholly owned subsidiary of Parent and shall succeed to and assume all of the rights and obligations of the Company and Merger Sub in accordance with the DGCL. The Merger shall have the effects set forth in Section 259 of the DGCL.

**Section 2.2 --- Effective Time**

On the Closing Date, the parties shall cause a Certificate of Merger, executed in accordance with the relevant provisions of the DGCL, to be filed with the Secretary of State of the State of Delaware. The Merger shall become effective upon such filing or at such later time as may be agreed by the parties and specified in the Certificate of Merger (the time at which the Merger becomes effective being referred to herein as the \"Effective Time\").

**Section 2.3 --- Effects of the Merger**

At the Effective Time:

> \(a\) the certificate of incorporation of the Surviving Corporation shall be amended and restated in its entirety as set forth in Exhibit A hereto, and as so amended and restated shall be the certificate of incorporation of the Surviving Corporation until thereafter amended in accordance with the DGCL and the terms of such certificate of incorporation;
>
> \(b\) the bylaws of the Surviving Corporation shall be amended and restated in their entirety as set forth in Exhibit B hereto, and as so amended and restated shall be the bylaws of the Surviving Corporation until thereafter amended in accordance with the DGCL, the certificate of incorporation of the Surviving Corporation, and the terms of such bylaws;
>
> \(c\) the directors and officers of Merger Sub immediately prior to the Effective Time shall become the directors and officers, respectively, of the Surviving Corporation, each to hold office in accordance with the certificate of incorporation and bylaws of the Surviving Corporation; and
>
> \(d\) the Merger shall constitute a \"change of control\" of the Company for all purposes, including under any agreements to which the Company or any Company Subsidiary is a party, including any operating agreement, joint venture agreement, license agreement, or similar arrangement to which the Company or any Company Subsidiary is bound.

**Section 2.4 --- Conversion of Shares**

At the Effective Time, by virtue of the Merger and without any action on the part of Parent, Merger Sub, the Company, or any holder of shares of the Company:

> \(a\) *Conversion of Company Common Stock.* Each share of common stock, par value \$0.001 per share, of the Company (the \"Company Common Stock\") issued and outstanding immediately prior to the Effective Time (other than Rollover Shares and Dissenting Shares, if any) shall be converted into the right to receive a portion of the Cash Consideration, calculated as such shareholder\'s Pro Rata Share multiplied by the Equity Value, payable in cash, less such shareholder\'s Pro Rata Share of the Escrow Amount.
>
> \(b\) *Conversion of Rollover Shares.* Each share of Company Common Stock that constitutes a \"Rollover Share\" (as defined below) shall be converted into the right to receive equity interests in Parent (or its designated acquisition vehicle) in accordance with the Chandra Rollover Agreement.
>
> \(c\) *Rollover Shares.* \"Rollover Shares\" means shares of Company Common Stock held by Dr. Naveen Chandra having an aggregate value equal to Thirty-Seven Million Dollars (\$37,000,000), representing approximately 10% of the Equity Value.
>
> \(d\) *Conversion of Merger Sub Stock.* Each share of common stock, par value \$0.01 per share, of Merger Sub issued and outstanding immediately prior to the Effective Time shall be converted into and become one (1) fully paid and non-assessable share of common stock, par value \$0.001 per share, of the Surviving Corporation.

**Section 2.5 --- Treatment of Restricted Stock**

All outstanding shares of restricted Company Common Stock (45,000 shares, representing approximately 2.3% of the Company\'s fully diluted equity) shall, immediately prior to the Effective Time, become fully vested in accordance with the terms of the Company\'s 2018 Equity Incentive Plan, which provides for acceleration of vesting upon a Change of Control (as defined therein). Each such vested share shall thereupon be treated as an outstanding share of Company Common Stock for all purposes under Section 2.4. The holders of such restricted stock are the eight (8) individuals set forth on Schedule 2.5 hereto (which schedule is not reproduced in these excerpts). The Company represents that each holder of restricted stock is an individual who is a citizen or resident of the United States and that a timely and valid election under Section 83(b) of the Code was filed with respect to each outstanding restricted stock grant.

**Section 2.6 --- Payment of Consideration; Consideration Waterfall**

The Merger Consideration shall be paid as follows:

> \(a\) *Funded Indebtedness Payoff.* At or immediately prior to the Closing, Parent shall cause all Funded Indebtedness of the Company and its Subsidiaries, in an aggregate amount of Forty-Eight Million Dollars (\$48,000,000), to be repaid in full in accordance with payoff letters delivered by the applicable lenders.

>
> \(f\) *Verification.* For the avoidance of doubt, the aggregate Merger Consideration is \$155,400,000 + \$37,000,000 + \$103,600,000 + \$74,000,000 = \$370,000,000, which equals the Equity Value.

**[ARTICLE VII --- TAX MATTERS]{.underline}**

**Section 7.1 --- Tax Returns for Pre-Closing Periods**

The Company shall prepare and timely file (or cause to be prepared and timely filed) all Tax returns of the Company and the Company Subsidiaries for all taxable periods ending on or before the Closing Date, including the Company\'s final S corporation return (IRS Form 1120-S) for the short taxable year ending on the Closing Date. Such Tax returns shall be prepared in a manner consistent with past practice unless otherwise required by applicable law or agreed to in writing by the parties. Parent shall be provided a draft of each such Tax return at least thirty (30) days prior to the due date (including extensions) for filing such return for review and comment. Parent shall notify the Shareholder Representative in writing of any comments or objections within fifteen (15) days of receipt, and the parties shall cooperate in good faith to resolve any disputes prior to filing.

**Section 7.2 --- Straddle Period Returns**

For any taxable period that includes but does not end on the Closing Date (a \"Straddle Period\"), the Company\'s items of income, gain, deduction, loss, and credit shall be allocated between the pre-Closing portion and the post-Closing portion of such Straddle Period using the closing-of-the-books method as of the end of the Closing Date, as if the Closing Date were the last day of such taxable period.

**Section 7.3 --- Section 338(h)(10) Election**

**Section 7.3(a) --- Election.** Parent and the Shareholders agree to jointly make an election under Section 338(h)(10) of the Code and Treasury Regulation Section 1.338(h)(10)-1 (the \"Section 338(h)(10) Election\") with respect to the acquisition of the Company stock pursuant to this Agreement. Each Shareholder agrees to execute IRS Form 8023 and any analogous or similar forms under applicable state or local Tax law, and to take all actions necessary to effectuate the Section 338(h)(10) Election. The parties acknowledge that, as a result of the Section 338(h)(10) Election, the acquisition of the Company stock shall be treated, for federal (and applicable state and local) income Tax purposes, as a deemed sale of all of the assets of the Company (and its disregarded subsidiaries) in a single transaction in exchange for the Aggregate Deemed Sale Price, followed by a deemed liquidation of the Company. All Shareholders (as listed on Schedule 1.1(a)) shall be required to consent to the Section 338(h)(10) Election as a condition to Closing.

**Section 7.3(b) --- Aggregate Deemed Sale Price; Allocation.** The \"Aggregate Deemed Sale Price\" or \"ADSP\" shall be determined in accordance with Treasury Regulation Section 1.338-4. The parties shall cooperate in good faith to allocate the ADSP among the assets of the Company (and its disregarded subsidiaries) in accordance with Section 1060 of the Code and the Treasury Regulations thereunder (the \"Allocation\"). Parent shall prepare a proposed Allocation within ninety (90) days following the Closing Date and deliver it to the Shareholder Representative for review. If the Shareholder Representative does not object in writing within thirty (30) days of receipt of the proposed Allocation, the Allocation shall become final and binding on all parties. If the Shareholder Representative objects in writing within such thirty (30) day period, the parties shall negotiate in good faith for an additional thirty (30) days to resolve the dispute, and if unable to do so, the dispute shall be resolved by a nationally recognized independent accounting firm mutually agreed upon by the parties.

The Allocation shall be determined using the residual method under Treasury Regulation Section 1.338-7 and shall allocate amounts among the following asset classes:

> • Class I --- Cash and cash equivalents;
>
> • Class II --- Actively traded personal property (within the meaning of Section 1092(d) of the Code);
>
> • Class III --- Accounts receivable, mortgages, and credit card receivables;
>
> • Class IV --- Inventory;
>
> • Class V --- All other tangible and intangible assets not described in Classes I through IV or Classes VI and VII;
>
> • Class VI --- Section 197 intangibles (other than goodwill and going concern value); and
>
> • Class VII --- Goodwill and going concern value.

Each party shall file all Tax returns (including IRS Form 8883) consistent with the final Allocation, and no party shall take any position on any Tax return, in any Tax proceeding, or otherwise that is inconsistent with the Allocation unless required to do so by a final determination (as defined in Section 1313 of the Code).

**Section 7.3(c) --- Cooperation; Filing Deadline.** The parties shall cooperate fully in the preparation and filing of all forms, returns, and elections necessary to effectuate the Section 338(h)(10) Election. The Section 338(h)(10) Election must be filed no later than the 15th day of the 9th month beginning after the month in which the Acquisition Date occurs. Based on the anticipated Closing Date of March 15, 2025, the filing deadline for IRS Form 8023 is December 15, 2025. Parent shall prepare and file IRS Form 8023, and each Shareholder shall execute such form in a timely manner and, in any event, no later than thirty (30) days prior to the applicable filing deadline.

**Section 7.4 --- Escrow; Tax Treatment of Escrow Amounts**

Twenty-Two Million Dollars (\$22,000,000) of the Cash Consideration shall be deposited at the Closing with First Atlantic Bank, N.A., as Escrow Agent, pursuant to the terms of the Escrow Agreement attached hereto as Exhibit E.

The Escrow Amount shall be held for a period of eighteen (18) months from the Closing Date (i.e., until September 15, 2026) to secure the indemnification obligations of the Shareholders under Article IX of this Agreement. During the escrow period, the Escrow Agent shall invest the Escrow Amount in accordance with the investment provisions of the Escrow Agreement. All interest or other earnings on the Escrow Amount shall be for the account of the Shareholders and shall be distributed to the Shareholders at the time of release of the Escrow Amount.

Upon expiration of the escrow period, the Escrow Agent shall release the Escrow Amount (less any amounts previously disbursed in satisfaction of indemnification claims or reserved for pending claims) to the Shareholders in accordance with their respective Pro Rata Shares.

**Tax Treatment.** For federal and applicable state income tax purposes, the Escrow Amount shall be treated as additional consideration received by the Shareholders in connection with the Merger. The parties intend that the Escrow Amount be reported as an amount realized by the Shareholders in the taxable year in which the Closing occurs, consistent with the treatment of the Escrow Amount as a portion of the Cash Consideration deposited with a third-party agent for the benefit of the Shareholders. Notwithstanding the foregoing, each Shareholder shall be solely responsible for determining the proper treatment of the Escrow Amount on such Shareholder\'s own Tax returns, and neither Parent nor the Surviving Corporation shall have any liability to any Shareholder with respect to the Tax treatment of the Escrow Amount.

**Section 7.5 --- Transfer Taxes**

All transfer, documentary, sales, use, stamp, registration, value added, and other similar Taxes and fees (including any penalties, interest, and additions to tax) incurred in connection with the Merger and the transactions contemplated hereby shall be borne and paid fifty percent (50%) by Parent and fifty percent (50%) by the Shareholders.

**Section 7.6 --- Tax Indemnification**

The Shareholders shall indemnify and hold harmless Parent, the Surviving Corporation, and their respective Affiliates from and against any and all Taxes of the Company or any Company Subsidiary attributable to any pre-Closing Tax period (including the pre-Closing portion of any Straddle Period), including any Taxes arising from the deemed asset sale under the Section 338(h)(10) Election that are the responsibility of the Shareholders under applicable law. This indemnification obligation shall be in addition to, and not in limitation of, the Shareholders\' indemnification obligations under Article IX.

\[Sections 7.7 (Tax Contests), 7.8 (Tax Refunds), and 7.9 (Tax Sharing Agreements) --- omitted.\]

\[Article VIII (Conditions to Closing) --- omitted except as excerpted below.\]

**[ARTICLE VIII --- CONDITIONS TO CLOSING (Selected Sections)]{.underline}**

**Section 8.1 --- Conditions to Obligations of All Parties**

The obligations of all parties to consummate the Merger and the other transactions contemplated by this Agreement are subject to the satisfaction (or waiver by all parties) of the following conditions:

> \(a\) No Governmental Authority of competent jurisdiction shall have enacted, issued, promulgated, enforced, or entered any law, order, decree, judgment, injunction, or ruling (whether temporary, preliminary, or permanent) that restrains, enjoins, or otherwise prohibits the consummation of the Merger or the other transactions contemplated hereby.
>
> \(b\) All required regulatory approvals, filings, and notifications shall have been obtained, made, or given, as applicable, and all applicable waiting periods shall have expired or been terminated.

**Section 8.2 --- Conditions to Obligations of Parent and Merger Sub**

The obligations of Parent and Merger Sub to consummate the Merger are subject to the satisfaction (or waiver by Parent) of the following additional conditions:

> \(a\) The representations and warranties of the Company set forth in this Agreement shall be true and correct in all material respects (or, with respect to representations and warranties that are qualified by \"materiality\" or \"Material Adverse Effect,\" in all respects) as of the Closing Date as though made on and as of such date (except to the extent such representations and warranties are made as of a specific earlier date, in which case they shall be true and correct as of such earlier date).
>
> \(b\) The Company shall have performed and complied in all material respects with all covenants and agreements required to be performed or complied with by it under this Agreement on or prior to the Closing Date.
>
> \(c\) The Company shall have maintained its status as a validly electing S corporation under Section 1361 of the Code continuously through the Closing Date.
>
> \(d\) All Shareholders shall have executed IRS Form 8023 and all other 338(h)(10) Forms necessary to effectuate the Section 338(h)(10) Election.
>
> \(e\) Dr. Naveen Chandra shall have executed and delivered the Chandra Rollover Agreement in substantially the form of Exhibit C and the Chandra Employment Agreement in substantially the form of Exhibit D.
>
> \(f\) Rachel Stowe shall have executed and delivered a consulting agreement with the Surviving Corporation for a term of two (2) years, in form and substance reasonably satisfactory to Parent.
>
> \(g\) All consents, waivers, and approvals of third parties required to be obtained in connection with the transactions contemplated hereby shall have been obtained, including any required consents under the operating agreement of Beacon Insights, LLC.
>
> \(h\) The financing contemplated by the Commitment Letter, dated December 15, 2024, from First Atlantic Bank, N.A. (comprising a Term Loan A facility in the aggregate principal amount of One Hundred Ninety Million Dollars (\$190,000,000) and a revolving credit facility in the aggregate principal amount of Thirty Million Dollars (\$30,000,000)) shall have been funded in accordance with the terms thereof.

**Section 8.3 --- Conditions to Obligations of the Company and Shareholders**

The obligations of the Company and the Shareholders to consummate the Merger are subject to the satisfaction (or waiver by the Shareholder Representative) of the following additional conditions:

> \(a\) The representations and warranties of Parent and Merger Sub set forth in this Agreement shall be true and correct in all material respects as of the Closing Date as though made on and as of such date.
>
> \(b\) Parent and Merger Sub shall have performed and complied in all material respects with all covenants and agreements required to be performed or complied with by them under this Agreement on or prior to the Closing Date.
>
> \(c\) Parent shall have deposited (or caused to be deposited) the Cash Consideration (including the Escrow Amount) with the Escrow Agent and the Paying Agent, as applicable, in accordance with Section 2.6.

\[Article IX --- Indemnification --- omitted. The Escrow Amount secures the Shareholders\' indemnification obligations under this Article.\]

\[Articles X (Termination), XI (Miscellaneous), and XII (Shareholder Representative) --- omitted.\]

**[SIGNATURE PAGE]{.underline}**
