**CALLOWAY, STERN & WHITAKER LLP** *Attorneys at Law*

301 South Tryon Street, Suite 4200 Charlotte, North Carolina 28202 Telephone: (704) 555-3800 \| Facsimile: (704) 555-3801

**PRIVILEGED AND CONFIDENTIAL --- ATTORNEY-CLIENT COMMUNICATION / ATTORNEY WORK PRODUCT** This memorandum is protected by the attorney-client privilege and the work product doctrine. It is intended solely for the use of the addressee. Any unauthorized disclosure, copying, or distribution is strictly prohibited.

**MEMORANDUM**

**TO:** Ridgeline Capital Partners Fund III, LP

> Attention: Fund III Investment Committee

**FROM:** James Calloway, Senior Tax Partner

> Calloway, Stern & Whitaker LLP Assisted by: Sandra Liu, Associate

**DATE:** February 10, 2025

**RE:** Tax Structure Memorandum --- Acquisition of Prism Health Analytics, Inc.

This memorandum sets forth our analysis of the federal and state income tax consequences arising from the proposed acquisition by Ridgeline Capital Partners Fund III, LP (\"Buyer\" or \"Fund III\") of Prism Health Analytics, Inc. (\"Prism\" or the \"Company\"). Our analysis is based upon our review of the Stock Purchase Agreement dated January 28, 2025, the quality of earnings and tax due diligence report prepared by Thornfield Accounting Group, LLP dated January 10, 2025, the capitalization table of Prism as of December 31, 2024, and related organizational and corporate documents provided to us by the parties and their respective advisors. We have assumed the accuracy and completeness of all factual information provided to us without independent verification. This memorandum addresses the federal and applicable state income tax consequences of the proposed transaction structure, including the contemplated election under Section 338(h)(10) of the Internal Revenue Code of 1986, as amended (the \"Code\"). All references to \"Section\" or \"Treas. Reg.\" are to provisions of the Code and the Treasury Regulations promulgated thereunder, respectively, unless otherwise indicated. Our state tax analysis is focused principally on North Carolina; California entity-level tax considerations are noted briefly.

Right-click to update Table of Contents

**[I. Executive Summary]{.underline}**

Ridgeline Capital Partners Fund III, LP (\"Buyer\") proposes to acquire one hundred percent (100%) of the outstanding stock of Prism Health Analytics, Inc. (\"Prism\" or the \"Company\"), a Delaware S corporation, through a reverse triangular merger. Ridgeline Acquisition Sub, Inc. (\"Merger Sub\"), a newly formed Delaware corporation and wholly owned subsidiary of Fund III, will merge with and into Prism, with Prism surviving the merger as a wholly owned subsidiary of Fund III.

The agreed-upon enterprise value for the transaction is \$425,000,000. After deducting approximately \$48,000,000 in funded debt at closing and approximately \$7,000,000 in seller transaction expenses, the equity value payable to the Prism shareholders is \$370,000,000. The parties have executed a Stock Purchase Agreement dated January 28, 2025, with an expected closing date of March 15, 2025.

Critically, the parties intend to make a joint election under Section 338(h)(10) of the Code to treat the stock acquisition as a deemed asset purchase for federal (and applicable state) income tax purposes. Based on our preliminary analysis, the Aggregate Deemed Sale Price (\"ADSP\") is \$473,000,000, and we have prepared a preliminary allocation of the ADSP among the seven asset classes prescribed by the residual method under Section 1060 and Treas. Reg. 1.338-7.

The Section 338(h)(10) election provides two primary benefits in this context. First, it provides Buyer with a stepped-up tax basis in all of Prism\'s assets equal to the allocated ADSP, generating substantial future depreciation and amortization deductions. Second, because Prism is an S corporation, the gain recognized on the deemed asset sale passes through to Prism\'s individual shareholders on their final S corporation K-1 schedules, rather than being subject to double taxation at both the corporate and shareholder level. No entity-level built-in gains tax under Section 1374 will apply because Prism\'s five-year recognition period following its conversion from C corporation status expired on December 31, 2021.

This memorandum is based upon our review of the Stock Purchase Agreement, the Thornfield Accounting Group tax due diligence report, the Company\'s capitalization table and corporate records, and discussions with the parties\' respective advisors. Our state tax analysis addresses North Carolina in detail and notes certain California entity-level tax considerations. We recommend that each shareholder consult with his or her individual tax advisor regarding state-specific filing obligations.

The sections that follow provide a detailed analysis of the transaction structure, the Section 338(h)(10) election mechanics, the purchase price allocation, the tax consequences to the selling shareholders and to Buyer, and the applicable state tax considerations.

**[II. Transaction Overview]{.underline}**

**[II.A. Parties]{.underline}**

The following is a summary of the principal parties to the transaction and the relevant entities, shareholders, and advisors.

**Buyer.** Ridgeline Capital Partners Fund III, LP (\"Fund III\" or \"Buyer\") is a Delaware limited partnership. The general partner of Fund III is Ridgeline Capital GP III, LLC, a Delaware limited liability company. Fund III is a mid-market private equity fund (vintage 2021) with \$1.4 billion in committed capital, focused on investments in the healthcare services and healthcare technology sectors.

**Merger Sub.** Ridgeline Acquisition Sub, Inc. (\"Merger Sub\") is a Delaware corporation newly formed and wholly owned by Fund III. Merger Sub was formed solely for purposes of effecting this acquisition and has no assets, operations, or liabilities other than those incident to its formation and the transactions contemplated by the Stock Purchase Agreement.

**Target.** Prism Health Analytics, Inc. (\"Prism\" or the \"Company\") is a Delaware S corporation. EIN: 81-2947365. Prism\'s principal office and headquarters are located at 4200 Lake Boone Trail, Suite 500, Raleigh, NC 27607. The Company is engaged in the business of providing healthcare data analytics, population health management software, and related advisory services to hospital systems, health plans, and government agencies.

**Prism Subsidiaries.** Prism has two subsidiaries:

> *Prism Data Services, LLC.* Prism Data Services, LLC is a North Carolina limited liability company, 100% owned by Prism. Prism Data Services is treated as a disregarded entity for federal income tax purposes under Treas. Reg. 1.301-7701-3 (default classification for a single-member LLC that has not elected to be treated as an association taxable as a corporation). EIN: 86-5512340 (disregarded; Prism Data Services uses Prism\'s EIN for federal tax reporting purposes). Prism Data Services holds certain software licenses, employs 22 individuals, and manages Prism\'s government-sector customer contracts, which generate approximately \$19,000,000 in annual revenue. Prism Data Services also holds North Carolina state data processing license No. DIT-2019-04821.
>
> *Beacon Insights, LLC.* Beacon Insights, LLC (\"Beacon\") is a North Carolina limited liability company taxed as a partnership for federal income tax purposes. EIN: 84-3219876. Prism holds 80.5% of the membership interests in Beacon. The remaining 19.5% is held by Marcus Delano (12.0%), who serves as Vice President of Data Engineering at Prism and is a North Carolina resident, and Shirin Avesta (7.5%), an outside investor and Virginia resident. Beacon generates approximately \$11,000,000 in annual revenue and approximately \$3,200,000 in EBITDA.

**Principal Shareholders of Prism.** As of the date hereof, the outstanding stock of Prism is held as follows:

> *Dr. Naveen Chandra.* Co-founder and Chief Executive Officer of Prism. Dr. Chandra holds 52% of the outstanding common stock. He is a North Carolina resident. Dr. Chandra will roll over \$37,000,000 of his equity consideration into the post-closing acquisition vehicle and will enter into a four-year employment agreement with new Prism upon closing.
>
> *Rachel Stowe.* Co-founder and Chief Technology Officer of Prism. Ms. Stowe holds 28% of the outstanding common stock. She is a North Carolina resident. Ms. Stowe will receive a full cash-out at closing and will enter into a two-year consulting agreement with new Prism.
>
> *Other Shareholders.* Fourteen (14) angel investors and early employees hold the remaining 20% of the outstanding common stock. All holders are individuals. Of these, eleven (11) reside in North Carolina, two (2) reside in California, and one (1) resides in New York.

**Restricted Stock.** Prism\'s equity incentive plan has granted restricted stock to eight (8) key employees. Total outstanding restricted stock: 45,000 shares, representing approximately 2.3% of the fully diluted equity of Prism. All grants were made subject to Section 83(b) elections filed by the recipients within 30 days of the respective grant dates. Vesting of all restricted stock accelerates upon a change of control, including the transaction contemplated herein. The 20% \"other shareholders\" allocation referenced above is inclusive of restricted stock holders.

**Prism Employees.** Prism has 310 total employees: 268 in North Carolina, 22 in Virginia, 12 in California, and 8 in New York. In addition to the Raleigh headquarters, Prism maintains offices at 580 Howard Street, Suite 310, San Francisco, California, and 110 East 42nd Street, 12th Floor, New York, New York.

**Advisors.** The principal advisors to the parties are as follows:

> • Calloway, Stern & Whitaker LLP --- Tax and deal counsel to Ridgeline Capital Partners.
>
> • Thornfield Accounting Group, LLP (Atlanta, GA) --- Quality of earnings and tax due diligence for Ridgeline.
>
> • Linden Rock Advisory, LLC --- Financial advisor to Prism.
>
> • First Atlantic Bank, N.A. --- Senior secured lender.

**[II.B. Transaction Structure]{.underline}**

The acquisition will be structured as a reverse triangular merger. Merger Sub, a newly formed Delaware corporation wholly owned by Fund III, will merge with and into Prism under the Delaware General Corporation Law. Prism will be the surviving entity in the merger and will become a direct, wholly owned subsidiary of Ridgeline Capital Partners Fund III, LP. The merger preserves Prism\'s legal existence, including its corporate charter, contracts, government licenses, and other legal relationships.

The merger constitutes a \"qualified stock purchase\" within the meaning of Section 338(d)(3) of the Code. In the merger, Buyer (through Merger Sub) acquires one hundred percent (100%) of the total voting power and one hundred percent (100%) of the total value of Prism\'s outstanding stock in a single transaction, thereby satisfying the statutory requirement that the purchasing corporation acquire at least 80% of the total voting power and at least 80% of the total value of the target corporation\'s stock during the 12-month acquisition period.

Upon completion of the merger, Prism\'s S corporation status will terminate on the closing date --- the date of the qualified stock purchase --- because a corporation (Prism, as a subsidiary of Fund III\'s organizational structure) will cease to have only eligible S corporation shareholders. Specifically, Fund III, a limited partnership, will become Prism\'s sole shareholder, and a partnership is not an eligible S corporation shareholder under Section 1361(b)(1)(B).

The parties will jointly elect under Section 338(h)(10) of the Code to treat the stock purchase as a deemed asset acquisition for federal income tax purposes. The legal, economic, and tax consequences of this election are discussed in detail in Section IV below.

Dr. Chandra\'s rollover equity will be contributed to Newco in exchange for membership interests. This rollover is expected to be completed in connection with the closing. Dr. Chandra will receive \$155,400,000 in cash consideration and will contribute \$37,000,000 to the post-closing equity vehicle. The precise mechanics and timing of the rollover are set forth in the Stock Purchase Agreement and related subscription documents.

All shareholders of Prism will be required to consent to the Section 338(h)(10) election as required under Treas. Reg. 1.338(h)(10)-1(c)(3). The Stock Purchase Agreement includes a condition to closing that all shareholders execute the required consent forms on or prior to the closing date.

**[II.C. Consideration and Financing]{.underline}**

The total consideration payable to the Prism shareholders in the merger is determined as follows:

  -----------------------------------------------------------------------
  **Component**                       **Amount**
  ----------------------------------- -----------------------------------
  Enterprise Value                    \$425,000,000

  Less: Funded Debt at Closing        (\$48,000,000)

  Less: Seller Transaction Expenses   (\$7,000,000)

  **Equity Value**                    **\$370,000,000**
  -----------------------------------------------------------------------

The equity consideration consists of cash and rollover equity as follows:

  -----------------------------------------------------------------------
  **Component**                       **Amount**
  ----------------------------------- -----------------------------------
  Cash at Closing                     \$333,000,000

  Rollover Equity (Dr. Chandra)       \$37,000,000

  **Total Equity Consideration**      **\$370,000,000**
  -----------------------------------------------------------------------

Reg. 1.338-4, ADSP should be computed starting from the amount realized on the stock ($370M) plus target liabilities. Thornfield's computation: $370M + $48M + $7M = $425M. This $48M ADSP overstatement directly inflates the Class VII goodwill residual in the memo.

The per-shareholder allocation of the equity consideration is as follows:

  -----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  **Shareholder**      **Ownership**   **Total Consideration**   **Cash**            **Rollover**
  -------------------- --------------- ------------------------- ------------------- ------------------
  Dr. Naveen Chandra   52%             \$192,400,000             \$155,400,000       \$37,000,000

  Rachel Stowe         28%             \$103,600,000             \$103,600,000       ---

  Other Shareholders   20%             \$74,000,000              \$74,000,000        ---

  **Total**            **100%**        **\$370,000,000**         **\$333,000,000**   **\$37,000,000**
  -----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Verification: \$155,400,000 + \$37,000,000 + \$103,600,000 + \$74,000,000 = \$370,000,000.

**Holdback/Escrow.** Pursuant to the Stock Purchase Agreement, \$22,000,000 of the cash consideration will be deposited in escrow with First Atlantic Bank, N.A. as escrow agent, to be held for eighteen (18) months following the closing date (anticipated release date: September 15, 2026), subject to reduction for valid indemnification claims by Buyer. The escrow is funded pro rata from all cash-receiving shareholders in proportion to their respective cash consideration amounts.

**Financing.** The acquisition financing consists of the following:

> *First Atlantic Bank, N.A.* is providing the following senior secured credit facilities:
>
> • Term Loan A: \$190,000,000 principal amount; 7-year maturity; interest at SOFR + 350 basis points. Fully funded at closing.
>
> • Revolving Credit Facility: \$30,000,000 commitment; 5-year maturity; interest at SOFR + 300 basis points. Unfunded at closing.
>
> • Total commitments: \$220,000,000; \$190,000,000 drawn at closing.

The equity contribution from Ridgeline is computed as follows: \$370,000,000 (total equity consideration) less \$37,000,000 (rollover equity from Dr. Chandra) less \$190,000,000 (funded debt) = \$143,000,000 in equity funded directly by Fund III.

**[III. Prism\'s Tax History and Status]{.underline}**

**[III.A. Corporate Tax History]{.underline}**

Prism Health Analytics, Inc. was incorporated in the State of Delaware on June 14, 2016. From the date of its incorporation through December 31, 2016 (a period of approximately six and one-half months), Prism was a C corporation for federal income tax purposes.

Effective January 1, 2017, Prism made an election under Section 1362(a) to be treated as an S corporation for federal income tax purposes. This election has been continuously maintained from January 1, 2017 through the date of this memorandum, a period of approximately eight years. Prism has filed S corporation returns (Form 1120-S) for each of the tax years 2017 through 2023. The 2024 return is currently in preparation.

We have reviewed the S election file and the annual shareholder records provided to us. No events causing a termination of the S election have been identified. All shareholders of Prism are U.S. individuals, and we are not aware of any ineligible shareholders. The equity incentive plan has resulted in the issuance of 45,000 shares of restricted stock to eight (8) key employees, and we note that Section 83(b) elections were timely filed for each grant. These shares are treated as outstanding for purposes of the S corporation shareholder requirements, and the restricted stock holders are reflected in the share counts described herein.

For the fiscal year ended December 31, 2024, Prism reported total revenue of approximately \$87,000,000.

**[III.B. Built-In Gains Analysis]{.underline}**

Section 1374 of the Code imposes an entity-level tax on the net recognized built-in gain of an S corporation during the recognition period following the corporation\'s conversion from C corporation status. The Protecting Americans from Tax Hikes Act of 2015 (the \"PATH Act\") permanently set the recognition period at five (5) years.

Prism\'s S corporation election became effective January 1, 2017. Accordingly, the five-year recognition period under Section 1374 commenced on January 1, 2017 and expired on December 31, 2021. The expected closing date for the transaction --- March 15, 2025 --- is well beyond the expiration of the recognition period.

At the time of Prism\'s S election (January 1, 2017), the net unrealized built-in gain (\"NUBIG\") was as follows:

  -----------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  **Component**                                   **Amount**
  ----------------------------------------------- -----------------------------------
  Fair Market Value of Assets (1/1/2017)          \$78,000,000

  Less: Aggregate Adjusted Tax Basis (1/1/2017)   (\$31,500,000)

  **NUBIG**                                       **\$46,500,000**
  -----------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Because the recognition period has expired, the deemed asset sale under the Section 338(h)(10) election will not trigger any entity-level built-in gains tax under Section 1374. The entirety of the deemed sale gain will pass through to the S corporation shareholders without an entity-level federal income tax.

For reference, Prism\'s current aggregate adjusted tax basis in its assets (as of the most recent available date) is approximately \$42,800,000, reflecting additions, dispositions, and depreciation deductions claimed since the S election became effective.

**[III.C. Subsidiaries and Affiliated Entities]{.underline}**

**Prism Data Services, LLC.** As noted in Section II.A, Prism Data Services, LLC is a North Carolina limited liability company wholly owned by Prism. Prism Data Services has not made an election under Treas. Reg. 1.301-7701-3 to be classified as an association taxable as a corporation; accordingly, as a single-member LLC, it is classified as a disregarded entity for federal income tax purposes. As a disregarded entity, the assets and liabilities of Prism Data Services are treated as assets and liabilities of Prism for federal income tax purposes, and all income and expenses flow directly to Prism. Prism Data Services holds software licenses, employs 22 individuals, and manages government-sector customer contracts generating approximately \$19,000,000 in annual revenue. Prism Data Services also holds North Carolina state data processing license No. DIT-2019-04821.

**Beacon Insights, LLC.** Beacon Insights, LLC is a North Carolina limited liability company that is treated as a partnership for federal income tax purposes. Prism holds an 80.5% membership interest. Marcus Delano, Vice President of Data Engineering at Prism, holds 12.0%, and Shirin Avesta, an outside investor and Virginia resident, holds 7.5%. Beacon generates approximately \$11,000,000 in annual revenue and approximately \$3,200,000 in EBITDA.

Prism\'s outside basis in its Beacon membership interest is \$6,400,000. The estimated fair market value of Prism\'s 80.5% interest is \$28,500,000. Prism\'s interest in Beacon Insights will be treated as an asset of Prism in the deemed asset sale under Section 338(h)(10), classified as a Class V asset as described in Section IV.C below.

Prism charges Beacon an annual management fee of \$850,000 for administrative, finance, and human resources services, and licenses certain proprietary intellectual property to Beacon for \$400,000 per year. These intercompany arrangements are described in the Beacon operating agreement and the related services and license agreements provided to us. The Beacon partnership agreement contains customary transfer restrictions, including a right of first refusal on transfers of membership interests.

**[III.D. R&D Tax Credits]{.underline}**

The Thornfield Accounting Group tax due diligence report identifies \$3,800,000 in unused federal research and development tax credits under Section 41 of the Code, carried forward from Prism\'s C corporation year (2016). Prism has also claimed approximately \$1,200,000 per year in North Carolina state R&D credits for qualifying research expenditures. These credits are noted and will be addressed in the post-closing tax planning.

**[IV. Section 338(h)(10) Election Analysis]{.underline}**

**[IV.A. Election Mechanics and Requirements]{.underline}**

Section 338(h)(10) of the Code permits the purchasing corporation and the selling shareholders (or, in the case of an S corporation target, the S corporation and all of its shareholders) to make a joint election to treat a qualified stock purchase as a deemed asset acquisition for federal income tax purposes. The election is governed by Treas. Reg. 1.338(h)(10)-1.

The effect of the election is that, for federal income tax purposes, the transaction is treated as if: (1) \"old Target\" sold all of its assets at fair market value in a single transaction at the close of the acquisition date (the \"deemed asset sale\"); and (2) \"old Target\" then distributed the deemed sale proceeds to its shareholders in complete liquidation (the \"deemed liquidation\"). No gain or loss is recognized on the actual sale of the stock; instead, the stock sale is disregarded, and the tax consequences flow entirely from the deemed asset sale and deemed liquidation.

For an S corporation target, the election is made jointly by the S corporation (and all of its shareholders) and the purchasing corporation. Pursuant to Treas. Reg. 1.338(h)(10)-1(c)(3), all shareholders must consent to the election. All Prism shareholders will be required to execute consent forms as a condition to closing. This requirement is reflected in Section 7.04 of the Stock Purchase Agreement.

The election must be made on Form 8023 (Elections Under Section 338 for Corporations Making Qualified Stock Purchases) no later than the 15th day of the 9th month beginning after the month in which the acquisition date falls. The acquisition date is expected to be March 15, 2025. The month of acquisition is March 2025. The 9th month beginning after March 2025 is December 2025. Accordingly, the filing deadline for Form 8023 is **December 15, 2025**.

**Consequences for Prism (Old Target).** Prism\'s S corporation status will terminate on the closing date when Buyer becomes the sole shareholder. For the deemed asset sale, old Prism is treated as recognizing gain or loss on the sale of all of its assets at fair market value in a single transaction occurring at the close of the acquisition date. Because Prism is an S corporation, the gain from the deemed asset sale passes through to the shareholders on their final S corporation returns. Prism will be required to file a short-period S corporation return (Form 1120-S) for the period January 1, 2025 through March 15, 2025, reporting the deemed sale gain and issuing Schedules K-1 to all shareholders.

**Consequences for Buyer (New Target).** Following the deemed asset sale and deemed liquidation, new Target is treated as a new corporation that purchased all of old Target\'s assets for the Aggregate Deemed Sale Price (\"ADSP\") on the day after the acquisition date. New Target receives a cost basis in each asset equal to the amount of ADSP allocated to that asset under the residual method, providing the stepped-up tax basis that is the principal tax benefit of the election for Buyer.

**[IV.B. Aggregate Deemed Sale Price (ADSP) Calculation]{.underline}**

Under Treas. Reg. 1.338-4, the Aggregate Deemed Sale Price (\"ADSP\") is the amount for which old Target is deemed to have sold all of its assets in the deemed asset sale. The ADSP is calculated as the sum of (i) the grossed-up amount realized on the sale of the target\'s stock and (ii) the liabilities of old target.

The ADSP is calculated as the enterprise value of Prism (\$425,000,000) plus the assumed liabilities of the Target (\$48,000,000 in funded debt at closing):

  ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  **Component**                                        **Amount**
  ---------------------------------------------------- -----------------------------------
  Enterprise Value                                     \$425,000,000

  Plus: Assumed Liabilities (Funded Debt at Closing)   \$48,000,000

  **ADSP**                                             **\$473,000,000**
  ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The ADSP of \$473,000,000 represents the total amount for which old Prism is deemed to have sold all of its assets in the deemed asset sale. This amount is then allocated among the asset classes using the residual method prescribed by Section 1060 and Treas. Reg. 1.338-7, as described in Section IV.C below.

The ADSP calculation reflects the full economic value attributable to Prism\'s assets, including the value inherent in the enterprise as a going concern, as well as the funded debt obligations that are assumed by the Buyer in connection with the transaction. The funded debt of \$48,000,000 is treated as a liability of old Target assumed in the deemed asset sale, consistent with Treas. Reg. 1.338-4(b)(2).

**[IV.C. Purchase Price Allocation Under Section 1060 / Residual Method]{.underline}**

Section 1060 of the Code and Treas. Reg. 1.338-7 require that the ADSP be allocated among the target\'s assets using the residual method. Under the residual method, the ADSP is allocated first to Class I assets (cash and cash equivalents) up to their fair market value, then to Class II assets, and so on through Class VI. Any residual amount is allocated to Class VII (goodwill and going concern value).

Based on our preliminary analysis and the asset information provided to us by the Company and by Thornfield Accounting Group, LLP, the proposed allocation is as follows:

**Class I --- Cash and Cash Equivalents:** \$12,400,000

> Represents cash and demand deposits held by Prism and Prism Data Services (as a disregarded entity) as of the anticipated closing date.

**Class II --- Actively Traded Personal Property, CDs, Government Securities:** \$0

> Prism does not hold any Class II assets.

**Class III --- Accounts Receivable, Mortgages, Credit Card Receivables:** \$24,300,000

> Represents accounts receivable from Prism\'s healthcare system and government agency customers, net of allowance for doubtful accounts.

**Class IV --- Inventory:** \$0

> Prism is a software and analytics company and does not hold physical inventory.

**Class V --- All Other Tangible and Intangible Assets Not in Classes VI or VII:** \$68,500,000

> The Class V allocation is comprised of the following:

  ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  **Asset**                                                    **Amount**
  ------------------------------------------------------------ -----------------------------------
  Real Property --- Raleigh Office Building (owned by Prism)   \$14,200,000

  Furniture, Fixtures, and Equipment                           \$6,800,000

  Software / Developed Technology                              \$19,000,000

  Prism\'s 80.5% Interest in Beacon Insights, LLC (FMV)        \$28,500,000

  **Total Class V**                                            **\$68,500,000**
  ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

> Prism\'s partnership interest in Beacon Insights, LLC is classified as a Class V asset at its estimated fair market value of \$28,500,000. This interest will transfer to new Target as part of the deemed asset acquisition.

**Class VI --- Section 197 Intangibles Other Than Goodwill/Going Concern Value:** \$95,000,000

> The Class VI allocation is comprised of the following:

  -----------------------------------------------------------------------
  **Asset**                           **Amount**
  ----------------------------------- -----------------------------------
  Customer Relationships              \$62,000,000

  Proprietary Data Sets / Databases   \$18,000,000

  Non-Compete Agreements              \$15,000,000

  **Total Class VI**                  **\$95,000,000**
  -----------------------------------------------------------------------

**Class VII --- Goodwill and Going Concern Value (Residual):**

  -----------------------------------------------------------------------
  **Component**                       **Amount**
  ----------------------------------- -----------------------------------
  Total ADSP                          \$473,000,000

  Less: Classes I through VI          (\$200,200,000)

  **Class VII Residual (Goodwill)**   **\$272,800,000**
  -----------------------------------------------------------------------

> Calculation of Classes I--VI total: \$12,400,000 + \$0 + \$24,300,000 + \$0 + \$68,500,000 + \$95,000,000 = \$200,200,000.

The foregoing allocation is preliminary and is subject to adjustment based upon the final asset valuation to be completed by Thornfield Accounting Group, LLP. The figures presented herein represent this firm\'s preliminary allocation based upon the information currently available.

We note that certain government contracts and licenses held by Prism Data Services may require notification to or consent from the applicable government authority in connection with the change of control. This is a commercial matter to be addressed by deal counsel in the pre-closing period.

**[V. Tax Consequences to Selling Shareholders]{.underline}**

**[V.A. General Framework --- Deemed Sale and Liquidation]{.underline}**

As described in Section IV.A above, the Section 338(h)(10) election causes old Prism to be treated as selling all of its assets in a single transaction at the close of the acquisition date (March 15, 2025) and then distributing the deemed sale proceeds to its shareholders in complete liquidation. The actual stock sale is disregarded for federal income tax purposes.

The total deemed sale gain is calculated as follows:

  ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  **Component**                                  **Amount**

**[ARTICLE I --- DEFINITIONS]{.underline}**

**Section 1.1 --- Defined Terms**

As used in this Agreement, the following terms shall have the meanings set forth below:

**\"Acquisition Date\"** means March 15, 2025 (or such other date on which the Closing occurs).

**\"ADSP\"** has the meaning set forth in Section 7.3(b).

**\"Beacon Insights\"** means Beacon Insights, LLC, a North Carolina limited liability company, EIN 84-3219876, classified as a partnership for federal income tax purposes, in which the Company holds an 80.5% membership interest.

**\"Cash Consideration\"** means an aggregate amount equal to Three Hundred Thirty-Three Million Dollars (\$333,000,000), payable to the Shareholders in accordance with Section 2.6.

**\"Chandra Employment Agreement\"** means the employment agreement between Dr. Naveen Chandra and the Surviving Corporation, in substantially the form attached hereto as Exhibit D.

**\"Chandra Rollover Agreement\"** means the Rollover Equity Contribution Agreement between Dr. Naveen Chandra and Parent, in substantially the form attached hereto as Exhibit C.

**\"Closing\"** means the closing of the transactions contemplated by this Agreement, as described in Section 3.1.

**\"Closing Date\"** means March 15, 2025, or such other date as may be mutually agreed by the parties.

**\"Company\"** means Prism Health Analytics, Inc., a Delaware S corporation, EIN 81-2947365, with its principal office at 4200 Lake Boone Trail, Suite 500, Raleigh, NC 27607.

**\"Company Subsidiary\"** means each of (a) Prism Data Services, LLC, a North Carolina limited liability company (EIN 86-5512340), which is treated as a disregarded entity for federal income tax purposes and is wholly owned by the Company, and (b) Beacon Insights, LLC (to the extent of the Company\'s 80.5% membership interest therein, it being understood that Beacon Insights is a separate legal entity classified as a partnership for federal income tax purposes).

**\"Enterprise Value\"** means Four Hundred Twenty-Five Million Dollars (\$425,000,000).

**\"Equity Value\"** means Three Hundred Seventy Million Dollars (\$370,000,000), calculated as Enterprise Value minus Funded Indebtedness minus Seller Transaction Expenses (i.e., \$425,000,000 − \$48,000,000 − \$7,000,000 = \$370,000,000).

**\"Escrow Agent\"** means First Atlantic Bank, N.A., 500 West Main Street, Durham, NC 27701.

**\"Escrow Amount\"** means Twenty-Two Million Dollars (\$22,000,000), which constitutes a portion of the Cash Consideration, to be deposited with the Escrow Agent at Closing pursuant to the Escrow Agreement.

**\"Escrow Release Date\"** means September 15, 2026 (eighteen (18) months following the Closing Date).

**\"Funded Indebtedness\"** means all outstanding indebtedness of the Company and its Subsidiaries for borrowed money as of the Closing, estimated to be Forty-Eight Million Dollars (\$48,000,000).

**\"Governmental License\"** means any license, permit, authorization, or approval issued by a Governmental Authority, including the NC Data Processing License.

**Section 2.2 --- Effective Time**

On the Closing Date, the parties shall cause a Certificate of Merger, executed in accordance with the relevant provisions of the DGCL, to be filed with the Secretary of State of the State of Delaware. The Merger shall become effective upon such filing or at such later time as may be agreed by the parties and specified in the Certificate of Merger (the time at which the Merger becomes effective being referred to herein as the \"Effective Time\").

**Section 2.3 --- Effects of the Merger**

At the Effective Time:

> \(a\) the certificate of incorporation of the Surviving Corporation shall be amended and restated in its entirety as set forth in Exhibit A hereto, and as so amended and restated shall be the certificate of incorporation of the Surviving Corporation until thereafter amended in accordance with the DGCL and the terms of such certificate of incorporation;
>
> \(b\) the bylaws of the Surviving Corporation shall be amended and restated in their entirety as set forth in Exhibit B hereto, and as so amended and restated shall be the bylaws of the Surviving Corporation until thereafter amended in accordance with the DGCL, the certificate of incorporation of the Surviving Corporation, and the terms of such bylaws;
>
> \(c\) the directors and officers of Merger Sub immediately prior to the Effective Time shall become the directors and officers, respectively, of the Surviving Corporation, each to hold office in accordance with the certificate of incorporation and bylaws of the Surviving Corporation; and
>
> \(d\) the Merger shall constitute a \"change of control\" of the Company for all purposes, including under any agreements to which the Company or any Company Subsidiary is a party, including any operating agreement, joint venture agreement, license agreement, or similar arrangement to which the Company or any Company Subsidiary is bound.

**Section 2.4 --- Conversion of Shares**

At the Effective Time, by virtue of the Merger and without any action on the part of Parent, Merger Sub, the Company, or any holder of shares of the Company:

> \(a\) *Conversion of Company Common Stock.* Each share of common stock, par value \$0.001 per share, of the Company (the \"Company Common Stock\") issued and outstanding immediately prior to the Effective Time (other than Rollover Shares and Dissenting Shares, if any) shall be converted into the right to receive a portion of the Cash Consideration, calculated as such shareholder\'s Pro Rata Share multiplied by the Equity Value, payable in cash, less such shareholder\'s Pro Rata Share of the Escrow Amount.
>
> \(b\) *Conversion of Rollover Shares.* Each share of Company Common Stock that constitutes a \"Rollover Share\" (as defined below) shall be converted into the right to receive equity interests in Parent (or its designated acquisition vehicle) in accordance with the Chandra Rollover Agreement.
>
> \(c\) *Rollover Shares.* \"Rollover Shares\" means shares of Company Common Stock held by Dr. Naveen Chandra having an aggregate value equal to Thirty-Seven Million Dollars (\$37,000,000), representing approximately 10% of the Equity Value.
>
> \(d\) *Conversion of Merger Sub Stock.* Each share of common stock, par value \$0.01 per share, of Merger Sub issued and outstanding immediately prior to the Effective Time shall be converted into and become one (1) fully paid and non-assessable share of common stock, par value \$0.001 per share, of the Surviving Corporation.

**Section 2.5 --- Treatment of Restricted Stock**

All outstanding shares of restricted Company Common Stock (45,000 shares, representing approximately 2.3% of the Company\'s fully diluted equity) shall, immediately prior to the Effective Time, become fully vested in accordance with the terms of the Company\'s 2018 Equity Incentive Plan, which provides for acceleration of vesting upon a Change of Control (as defined therein). Each such vested share shall thereupon be treated as an outstanding share of Company Common Stock for all purposes under Section 2.4. The holders of such restricted stock are the eight (8) individuals set forth on Schedule 2.5 hereto (which schedule is not reproduced in these excerpts). The Company represents that each holder of restricted stock is an individual who is a citizen or resident of the United States and that a timely and valid election under Section 83(b) of the Code was filed with respect to each outstanding restricted stock grant.

**Section 2.6 --- Payment of Consideration; Consideration Waterfall**

The Merger Consideration shall be paid as follows:

> \(a\) *Funded Indebtedness Payoff.* At or immediately prior to the Closing, Parent shall cause all Funded Indebtedness of the Company and its Subsidiaries, in an aggregate amount of Forty-Eight Million Dollars (\$48,000,000), to be repaid in full in accordance with payoff letters delivered by the applicable lenders.
>
> \(b\) *Seller Transaction Expenses.* At the Closing, Seven Million Dollars (\$7,000,000) in Seller Transaction Expenses shall be paid from the proceeds otherwise payable to the Shareholders (or, at the Company\'s election, directly by the Company immediately prior to the Closing), including advisory fees of Three Million Five Hundred Thousand Dollars (\$3,500,000) payable to Linden Rock Advisory, LLC, legal fees of Two Million Two Hundred Thousand Dollars (\$2,200,000) payable to Calloway, Stern & Whitaker LLP (as Company counsel), and other transaction-related costs of One Million Three Hundred Thousand Dollars (\$1,300,000).

The Memo prepared by Calloway, Stern & Whitaker LLP ("CSW") analyzes a
reverse triangular merger in which Ridgeline Capital Partners Fund III,
LP ("Fund III" or "Buyer") acquires 100% of Prism Health Analytics,
Inc. ("Prism"), a Delaware S corporation, followed by a joint Section
338(h)(10) election. The Memo's core conclusions --- that the structure
yields a stepped-up basis for Buyer, that no Section 1374 built-in gains
tax applies, and that the deemed sale gain passes through to the S
corporation shareholders without entity-level federal tax --- are
directionally sound **if** the threshold factual assumptions hold.
Cross-checking the Memo against the six supporting deal documents,
however, reveals a number of material problems that, in several cases,
undermine or contradict the Memo's own conclusions.

**Three findings are Critical.** First, the Memo asserts that "no events
causing a termination of the S election have been identified" and that
"all shareholders of Prism are U.S. individuals" with no ineligible
shareholders --- a statement directly contradicted by the Thornfield
Accounting Group, LLP ("Thornfield") tax due diligence report (which
flags two unconfirmed restricted-stock holders as a *critical* open
item) and by the Prism capitalization table (which flags *three*
unconfirmed holders, including an H-1B visa holder whose
substantial-presence-test compliance is unverified). Validity of the S
election is a threshold requirement for the Section 338(h)(10) election;
if the election was inadvertently terminated, the entire structure
collapses. Second, the Memo's Aggregate Deemed Sale Price ("ADSP") of
\$473,000,000 double-counts the \$48,000,000 funded debt (enterprise
value already includes the debt), overstating the ADSP by approximately
\$48,000,000 --- an error Thornfield's own preliminary purchase price
allocation expressly flags as a "SIGNIFICANT DISCREPANCY." Third, the
Memo omits any analysis of Section 1202 qualified small business stock
("QSBS"), even though CSW expressly promised that analysis in writing
(November 20, 2024), the selling shareholders' advisor repeatedly and
urgently requested it, and the interaction between QSBS and the Section
338(h)(10) deemed asset sale may eliminate a 100% federal gain exclusion
worth seven figures to multiple shareholders.

**Seven findings are High severity.** These include: (i) the Memo's
escrow tax-treatment analysis (installment deferral to 2026) directly
contradicts the Stock Purchase Agreement's express provision requiring
the escrow to be reported as amount realized in the year of closing;
(ii) the Memo's aggregate adjusted tax basis of \$42,800,000 does not
reconcile to the asset valuation summary, whose per-asset tax bases sum
to approximately \$64,300,000 (a \$21,500,000 gap that overstates the
deemed sale gain); (iii) the Memo understates Class V developed
technology/software by \$28,500,000 (\$19,000,000 vs. Thornfield's
\$47,500,000), shifting deductions from 5--7 year amortization into
15-year goodwill; (iv) the Memo fails to disclose that the Beacon
Insights, LLC IP License Agreement terminates *automatically* upon the
change of control; (v) the Memo assumes the North Carolina data
processing license "should remain valid" despite the Stock Purchase
Agreement and asset valuation confirming it is non-transferable and
non-assignable, putting \~\$19,000,000 of annual government revenue at
risk; (vi) the Memo does not analyze the risk that \$3,800,000 of
suspended C-corporation R&D credits are permanently lost in the deemed
liquidation; and (vii) the Memo omits the \~\$6,300,000 cumulative
Section 482 transfer-pricing exposure on the Prism/Beacon intercompany
fees that Thornfield flagged.

**Twelve findings are Medium severity** and **fifteen are Low
severity**, addressing the Beacon right of first refusal, Section 751
"hot assets," the Section 754 election, AAA/AE&P distribution ordering,
the use of basic rather than fully-diluted ownership for gain
allocation, a shareholder-count discrepancy, the rollover tax-funding
gap, state-tax gaps (including an unaddressed New York sales-tax
exposure), and a series of citation and date errors.

In total, the review identifies **37 distinct issues**. The most
consequential cluster around (a) the validity of the S corporation
election, (b) the ADSP and purchase price allocation math, and (c) the
QSBS omission --- each of which should be resolved before the Form 8023
election is filed and, in the case of the S election and QSBS issues,
before the parties treat the structure as final.

## II. Scope and Documents Reviewed

**Severity: Critical**

**The issue.** The Memo states (Section III.A) that "No events causing a
termination of the S election have been identified. All shareholders of
Prism are U.S. individuals, and we are not aware of any ineligible
shareholders." This assertion is contradicted by two supporting
documents:

-   The **Thornfield Tax Due Diligence Report** (Section III.B, and
    Executive Summary Finding 1) identifies a **critical** open item: S
    corporation eligibility could not be confirmed for **two of the
    eight** restricted-stock holders --- (a) one holder who appears to
    be a Canadian national on an H-1B visa whose
    substantial-presence-test compliance under Section 7701(b)(3) was
    not verified for all years, and (b) one holder whose shares may have
    been transferred to a "Family Holdings, LLC," which would be an
    ineligible shareholder under Section 1361(b)(1). Thornfield warns
    that if either condition exists, the S election was retroactively
    terminated under Section 1362(d)(2), with "cascading consequences
    including entity-level federal and state income tax liability for
    all open years, potential penalties and interest, and the
    unavailability of the Section 338(h)(10) election."
-   The **Prism Capitalization Table** flags **three** restricted-stock
    holders with "Unconfirmed" S corporation eligibility: **Lin Wei
    Zhang** (H-1B visa holder, citizen of the People's Republic of
    China, residency/citizenship not verified), **Yusuf Al-Rashidi**
    (dual U.S./Jordan citizen, U.S. citizenship documentation not on
    file), and **Nina Petrova** (Bulgarian-born,
    green-card/lawful-permanent-resident status not verified). The cap
    table's aggregate note confirms "Unverified S corp eligibility: 3
    shareholders."

**Additional inconsistency.** Thornfield describes the H-1B holder as a
"Canadian national," while the cap table identifies Lin Wei Zhang as a
"PRC citizen." The underlying fact is itself unresolved.

**Why it is critical.** A valid S corporation election is a threshold
requirement for a Section 338(h)(10) election. The Memo's entire
analysis --- no entity-level tax, gain pass-through to shareholders, no
Section 1374 built-in gains tax --- depends on continuous S status from
January 1, 2017. The Memo's blanket statement that there are no
ineligible shareholders is unsupported and contradicts the record. The
Stock Purchase Agreement (the "SPA") compounds the problem: Section
4.9(a) represents unqualifiedly that "each shareholder is an eligible S
corporation shareholder"; Section 2.5 represents that each
restricted-stock holder "is an individual who is a citizen or resident
of the United States"; and Section 8.2(c) makes "maintained its status
as a validly electing S corporation ... continuously through the Closing
Date" a closing condition. If the S election was inadvertently
terminated, these representations are breached and the closing condition
cannot be satisfied.

**Recommendation.** Obtain definitive immigration/residency
documentation for Lin Wei Zhang (substantial-presence test for every
year since grant), documentation of U.S. citizenship for Yusuf
Al-Rashidi, documentation of lawful-permanent-resident status for Nina
Petrova, and documentation regarding the alleged transfer to a family
LLC. Confirm eligibility of every shareholder for every year since
January 1, 2017, before relying on the Section 338(h)(10) structure. The
Memo should be revised to disclose this as an open critical item rather
than asserting no ineligible shareholders exist.

#### C-2. ADSP Calculation Double-Counts the \$48,000,000 Funded Debt

**Severity: High**

**The issue.** The Beacon Insights, LLC Operating Agreement (Section
9.02) provides that "The IP License Agreement shall terminate
automatically upon a Transfer of Prism's Membership Interest in the
Company (including a deemed Transfer resulting from a Change of Control
of Prism), unless the parties agree otherwise in writing." The merger is
a Change of Control of Prism (Section 8.03(a)(ii) and the Change of
Control definition), and the Section 338(h)(10) deemed asset sale is a
deemed transfer of Prism's Beacon interest. The IP License Agreement
therefore **terminates automatically at closing** unless the parties
agree otherwise in writing.

The Memo (Section VIII) does not mention this automatic termination.
Beacon's business relies on the licensed IP --- Beacon generates
approximately \$11,000,000 in annual revenue using the proprietary
algorithms, software platforms, and data-processing tools licensed from
Prism (Beacon OA Section 9.02). If the license terminates at closing,
Beacon's ability to operate is impaired, which directly affects the
\$28,500,000 value assigned to Prism's 80.5% Beacon interest (Class V)
and the going-concern assumptions underlying the overall enterprise
value.

**Recommendation.** Confirm whether the parties intend to enter a new or
continued IP license agreement with Beacon post-closing (requiring
Majority Interest consent under Beacon OA Section 9.03, i.e., Prism/new
Target's 80.5%). If no agreement is reached, re-evaluate the Beacon
interest valuation and the government/analytics revenue assumptions. The
Memo should disclose the automatic termination and its consequences.

#### H-5. North Carolina Data Processing License Is Non-Transferable --- Memo Assumes It "Should Remain Valid"

**Severity: High**

**The issue.** The Memo (Sections VI.A and IX.A) states that "we expect
that the existing license should remain valid post-closing, subject to
confirmation with the North Carolina Department of Information
Technology," and treats the matter as a minor commercial item. The
record contradicts this comfortable assumption:

-   **SPA Section 4.9(i)** represents that the NC Data Processing
    License (No. DIT-2019-04821) held by Prism Data Services, LLC "is
    non-transferable and non-assignable" per its terms.
-   The **Asset Valuation Summary** (A-008 and Note 3) assigns the
    license **\$0 fair market value** precisely because it "is
    non-transferable and non-assignable per license terms. In a deemed
    asset sale under §338(h)(10), this license cannot be transferred and
    may need to be re-obtained by the surviving entity." Thornfield
    warns that "if the license is deemed terminated upon the §338(h)(10)
    deemed asset sale, the ongoing government-sector revenue (\~\$19M)
    attributable to the license may be impaired, affecting the overall
    enterprise value and PPA."

Prism Data Services' government-sector business generates approximately
\$19,000,000 in annual revenue that "partially depends on this license"
(Asset Valuation A-008). The Memo does not address the
non-transferability, the \$0 valuation, the possible need to re-obtain
the license, or the revenue-impairment risk.

**Recommendation.** Obtain a definitive ruling/confirmation from the NC
Department of Information Technology on whether the license survives the
reverse triangular merger / change of control or must be re-obtained. If
it does not survive, re-evaluate the government-sector revenue
assumptions and the PPA. The Memo should not assume the license "should
remain valid" in the face of express non-transferability.

#### H-6. \$3,800,000 Suspended C-Corporation R&D Credits --- Risk of Permanent Loss Not Analyzed

#### M-13. Counsel-Role Inconsistency: Memo Says CSW Is Buyer's Counsel; SPA Pays CSW "as Company Counsel"

**Severity: Medium**

**The issue.** The Memo is addressed "TO: Ridgeline Capital Partners
Fund III, LP" "FROM: James Calloway, Senior Tax Partner, Calloway, Stern
& Whitaker LLP," and the Memo's advisor list (Section II.A) describes
"Calloway, Stern & Whitaker LLP --- Tax and deal counsel to Ridgeline
Capital Partners." The Thornfield report likewise states it was engaged
"through its tax counsel Calloway, Stern & Whitaker LLP." In his
November 20, 2024 email, Mr. Calloway himself writes: "I recognize that
our primary engagement is with Ridgeline as the buyer, and the selling
shareholders are not our clients."

The Stock Purchase Agreement says the opposite. **SPA Section 2.6(b)**
provides that the \$7,000,000 in seller transaction expenses includes
"legal fees of Two Million Two Hundred Thousand Dollars (\$2,200,000)
payable to Calloway, Stern & Whitaker LLP **(as Company counsel)**."
"Company" is defined in SPA Section 1.1 as Prism Health Analytics, Inc.
--- the seller/target, not the buyer. Seller transaction expenses are
borne by the selling shareholders (SPA Section 2.6(b)). CSW is therefore
being paid \$2,200,000 as Prism's (the seller's) counsel, out of the
sellers' proceeds, while simultaneously representing to Ridgeline (the
buyer) that it is the buyer's tax and deal counsel and producing a
privileged memo addressed to the buyer.

**Why it matters.** This is not a tax-math error, but it is a
significant inconsistency that bears on (a) the scope and reliability of
the Memo (whose client is CSW actually advising --- the buyer or the
seller?), (b) the attorney-client privilege analysis attaching to the
Memo, and (c) potential conflict-of-interest concerns where the same
firm purports to represent both sides of a transaction. The Memo's
preamble asserts the attorney-client privilege "for the sole use of"
Ridgeline, yet the SPA compensates CSW as the Company's counsel. The
parties and the investment committee should clarify CSW's engagement
scope and confirm the Memo's intended beneficiary and privilege
position.

**Recommendation.** Clarify and document CSW's engagement (buyer
counsel, seller counsel, or both with informed consent/waivers). Confirm
the privilege analysis for the Memo. If CSW represents both sides,
confirm that appropriate conflict waivers were obtained.

#### M-14. Memo's "Items Noted for Further Analysis" (Section IX.C) and Conclusions (Section X) Omit Every Critical and High-Severity Open Item

**Severity: Medium**

**The issue.** The Memo's Section IX.C ("Items Noted for Further
Analysis") lists only four items: (1) final asset valuation from
Thornfield; (2) confirm shareholder consents for the 338(h)(10)
election; (3) coordinate with employment counsel on the Chandra
employment and Stowe consulting agreements; and (4) review post-closing
entity structure. The Memo's Section X ("Conclusions and
Recommendations") reaches seven conclusions, all favorable, and none
flag any open risk.

Neither section mentions any of the critical or high-severity open items
that the supporting documents raise and that this review identifies:
S-election eligibility (C-1), the ADSP double-count (C-2), QSBS (C-3),
the escrow/SPA conflict (H-1), the basis reconciliation gap (H-2), the
Class V software understatement (H-3), the Beacon IP license
auto-termination (H-4), the NC license non-transferability (H-5), the
\$3,800,000 R&D-credit loss risk (H-6), the \$6,300,000 Section 482
transfer-pricing exposure (H-7), the New York sales-tax exposure (M-9),
or the Section 751/754 Beacon issues (M-2, M-3). The Memo's open-items
list and conclusions therefore present an incomplete and unduly
optimistic picture of the transaction's tax risks, omitting the very
items the diligence record flags as most consequential.

**Recommendation.** Revise Section IX.C to include all open items
identified in the diligence record, and revise Section X to condition

{"file_path": "output/audit_working.md", "new_string": "California-resident shareholders and for the entity-level tax.

#### M-13. Counsel-Role Inconsistency: Memo Says CSW Is Buyer's Counsel; SPA Pays CSW "as Company Counsel"

**Severity: Medium**

**The issue.** The Memo is addressed "TO: Ridgeline Capital Partners
Fund III, LP" "FROM: James Calloway, Senior Tax Partner, Calloway, Stern
& Whitaker LLP," and the Memo's advisor list (Section II.A) describes
"Calloway, Stern & Whitaker LLP --- Tax and deal counsel to Ridgeline
Capital Partners." The Thornfield report likewise states it was engaged
"through its tax counsel Calloway, Stern & Whitaker LLP." In his
November 20, 2024 email, Mr. Calloway himself writes: "I recognize that
our primary engagement is with Ridgeline as the buyer, and the selling
shareholders are not our clients."

The Stock Purchase Agreement says the opposite. **SPA Section 2.6(b)**
provides that the \$7,000,000 in seller transaction expenses includes
"legal fees of Two Million Two Hundred Thousand Dollars (\$2,200,000)
payable to Calloway, Stern & Whitaker LLP **(as Company counsel)**."
"Company" is defined in SPA Section 1.1 as Prism Health Analytics, Inc.
--- the seller/target, not the buyer. Seller transaction expenses are
borne by the selling shareholders (SPA Section 2.6(b)). CSW is therefore
being paid \$2,200,000 as Prism's (the seller's) counsel, out of the
sellers' proceeds, while simultaneously representing to Ridgeline (the
buyer) that it is the buyer's tax and deal counsel and producing a
privileged memo addressed to the buyer.

**Why it matters.** This is not a tax-math error, but it is a
significant inconsistency that bears on (a) the scope and reliability of
the Memo (whose client is CSW actually advising --- the buyer or the
seller?), (b) the attorney-client privilege analysis attaching to the
Memo, and (c) potential conflict-of-interest concerns where the same
firm purports to represent both sides of a transaction. The Memo's
preamble asserts the attorney-client privilege "for the sole use of"
Ridgeline, yet the SPA compensates CSW as the Company's counsel. The
parties and the investment committee should clarify CSW's engagement
scope and confirm the Memo's intended beneficiary and privilege
position.

**Recommendation.** Clarify and document CSW's engagement (buyer
counsel, seller counsel, or both with informed consent/waivers). Confirm
the privilege analysis for the Memo. If CSW represents both sides,
confirm that appropriate conflict waivers were obtained.

#### M-14. Memo's "Items Noted for Further Analysis" (Section IX.C) and Conclusions (Section X) Omit Every Critical and High-Severity Open Item

**Severity: Medium**

**The issue.** The Memo's Section IX.C ("Items Noted for Further
Analysis") lists only four items: (1) final asset valuation from
Thornfield; (2) confirm shareholder consents for the 338(h)(10)
election; (3) coordinate with employment counsel on the Chandra
employment and Stowe consulting agreements; and (4) review post-closing
entity structure. The Memo's Section X ("Conclusions and
Recommendations") reaches seven conclusions, all favorable, and none
flag any open risk.

Neither section mentions any of the critical or high-severity open items
that the supporting documents raise and that this review identifies:
S-election eligibility (C-1), the ADSP double-count (C-2), QSBS (C-3),
the escrow/SPA conflict (H-1), the basis reconciliation gap (H-2), the
Class V software understatement (H-3), the Beacon IP license
auto-termination (H-4), the NC license non-transferability (H-5), the
\$3,800,000 R&D-credit loss risk (H-6), the \$6,300,000 Section 482
transfer-pricing exposure (H-7), the New York sales-tax exposure (M-9),
or the Section 751/754 Beacon issues (M-2, M-3). The Memo's open-items
list and conclusions therefore present an incomplete and unduly
optimistic picture of the transaction's tax risks, omitting the very
items the diligence record flags as most consequential.

**Recommendation.** Revise Section IX.C to include all open items
identified in the diligence record, and revise Section X to condition
the favorable conclusions on resolution of the S-election, ADSP, QSBS,
transfer-pricing, R&D-credit, and license items.

### D. Low-Severity Findings", "old_string": "California-resident shareholders and for the entity-level tax.

### D. Low-Severity Findings"}

#### L-16. 2024 Form 1120-S Due March 15, 2025 (Same Day as Closing) --- Not Addressed

The Thornfield report (Section IV.A) states that the 2024 Form 1120-S
has not yet been filed and that its filing deadline is **March 15,
2025**, which "coincides with the expected transaction closing date,"
and that "an extension of time to file will likely be required." The
Memo (Section III.A) notes only that "The 2024 return is currently in
preparation," and the Memo's post-closing filings list (Section IX.B)
addresses only the final short-period return for January 1 -- March 15,
2025 --- it does not address the 2024 full-year return, its
same-day-as-closing deadline, or the likely need for an extension. The
2024 return must be filed or extended on or before March 15, 2025, and
its preparation should be coordinated with the closing to avoid a
late-filing penalty and to ensure the 2024 K-1s are issued correctly.

#### L-17. North Carolina R&D Credits Lack a Formal Third-Party Credit Study

The Thornfield report (Section IV.C and Summary Finding 6) states that
Prism claims approximately \$1,200,000 per year in North Carolina R&D
credits but "relies on internal calculations rather than a formal,
third-party R&D credit study," and that "in the event of a state audit,
the absence of a contemporaneous credit study could increase the risk of
disallowance." Thornfield recommends obtaining a formal Section 41 /
North Carolina R&D credit study. The Memo (Section III.D) mentions the
North Carolina R&D credits but does not flag the absence of a formal
study or the audit-disallowance risk. This exposure survives closing and
is relevant to the seller's indemnity obligations.

#### L-18. Funded Debt Characterized as "Assumed by the Buyer" (Memo) vs. "Repaid in Full" (SPA)

The Memo (Section IV.B) states that the ADSP reflects "the funded debt
obligations that are **assumed by the Buyer** in connection with the
transaction" and that "The funded debt of \$48,000,000 is treated as a
liability of old Target **assumed** in the deemed asset sale." The SPA
(Section 2.6(a)) provides the opposite: "Parent shall cause all Funded
Indebtedness of the Company and its Subsidiaries ... **to be repaid in
full** in accordance with payoff letters delivered by the applicable
lenders." The existing debt is repaid (not assumed) at closing. While
this characterization difference does not change the ADSP computation
(target liabilities are included in ADSP whether assumed or paid off),
the Memo's factual statement that the debt is "assumed by the Buyer" is
inaccurate per the SPA and should be corrected.

#### L-19. \$58,000,000 EBITDA Figure (Memo Section VII.B) Is Uncorroborated and Appears High

The Memo (Section VII.B) states that "Prism's historical EBITDA of
approximately \$58,000,000 (2024 estimate)" supports the conclusion that
new Target has sufficient adjusted taxable income to deduct the
anticipated interest expense under Section 163(j). No other document in
the record states Prism's EBITDA. The Memo elsewhere (Section III.A)
reports 2024 revenue of approximately \$87,000,000; a \$58,000,000
EBITDA figure implies a **\~67% EBITDA margin**, which is unusually high
for a healthcare data-analytics/software company (Beacon's own EBITDA
margin, by comparison, is \~29% on \$11,000,000 of revenue). The
Section 163(j) interest-deductibility conclusion depends on this
figure: at \$190,000,000 of Term Loan A debt at SOFR + 350 bps, annual
interest is roughly \$15,000,000, and 30% of \$58,000,000 EBITDA is
\$17,400,000 --- just enough capacity. If EBITDA is materially lower,
the interest may not be fully deductible. The EBITDA figure should be
corroborated with Thornfield's quality-of-earnings analysis.

#### L-20. Tax Indemnification Structure (SPA Section 7.6) Not Addressed

SPA Section 7.6 provides that the Shareholders indemnify Parent, the
Surviving Corporation, and their affiliates against "any and all Taxes
of the Company or any Company Subsidiary attributable to any pre-Closing
Tax period ... including any Taxes arising from the deemed asset sale
under the Section 338(h)(10) Election that are the responsibility of the
Shareholders under applicable law." The Memo does not address this
indemnity structure. It is directly relevant to the risk allocation for
the Section 482 transfer-pricing exposure (H-7, \~\$6,300,000), the New
York sales-tax exposure (M-9, \$150,000--\$300,000), the NC R&D-credit
disallowance risk (L-17), and any pre-closing tax liability arising if
the S election is invalidated (C-1). The Memo should cross-reference the
indemnity and confirm that the escrow (\$22,000,000) is sized to cover
these exposures.

#### L-21. SPA Allocation-Finalization Procedure (Section 7.3(b)) Not Addressed

SPA Section 7.3(b) establishes a contractual procedure for finalizing
the purchase price allocation: Parent prepares a proposed allocation
within **90 days** following the closing date; the Shareholder
Representative has **30 days** to object; the parties negotiate for an
additional **30 days**; and if unresolved, the dispute is resolved by a
"nationally recognized independent accounting firm." The Memo (Section
IV.C) presents its allocation as "preliminary and subject to adjustment
based upon the final asset valuation to be completed by Thornfield" but
does not mention the SPA's 90/30/30-day finalization procedure or the
independent-firm dispute-resolution mechanism. Given that the Memo and
Thornfield currently disagree by \$76,500,000 in goodwill (C-2, H-3),
this procedure is likely to be invoked; the Memo should describe it.

#### L-22. Shareholder Form 8023 Execution Deadline Is 30 Days Before the Filing Deadline (November 15, 2025), Not December 15, 2025

The Memo (Section IV.A and Appendix A) states that the Form 8023 filing
deadline is December 15, 2025. SPA Section 7.3(c) additionally requires
that "each Shareholder shall execute such form in a timely manner and,
in any event, **no later than thirty (30) days prior to the applicable
filing deadline**" --- i.e., by **November 15, 2025**. The Memo does not
mention this earlier shareholder-execution deadline. Given the
shareholder-consent collection effort required (especially for the
unverified-eligibility holders in C-1), the November 15, 2025 deadline
is the operative date for shareholder action and should be flagged.

#### L-23. Transfer Taxes (SPA Section 7.5) Not Addressed in the State-Tax Analysis

SPA Section 7.5 provides that "All transfer, documentary, sales, use,
stamp, registration, value added, and other similar Taxes and fees ...
incurred in connection with the Merger ... shall be borne and paid fifty
percent (50%) by Parent and fifty percent (50%) by the Shareholders."
The Memo's state-tax analysis (Section VI) does not address transfer or
documentary taxes or the 50/50 allocation. While Delaware imposes no
stock-transfer tax, North Carolina and other states may impose
documentary or transfer taxes on the merger consideration; the Memo
should address this dimension and the contractual allocation.

#### L-24. Beacon Tax-Distribution Obligation and BBA Partnership-Representative / Push-Out Election Not Addressed

The Beacon Operating Agreement (Section 7.02) requires Beacon to
distribute to each member, within 90 days after each fiscal year end, an
amount equal to the member's share of Beacon taxable income multiplied
by the highest combined federal/state marginal rate for North Carolina
residents. Section 11.02 designates Prism as the "Partnership
Representative" under the BBA partnership-audit regime (Section 6223),
with authority to bind the company and members in tax audits, and
Section 11.04 authorizes a Section 6226 push-out election. After the
change of control, new Target (Prism) remains the Partnership
Representative and 80.5% member. The Memo (Section VIII) does not
address (a) Beacon's tax-distribution obligation as it affects the
post-closing owner, (b) the BBA audit-regime implications (including
that the Partnership Representative can bind the minority members
Delano and Avesta), or (c) the push-out election decision. These are
post-closing operational and audit-risk matters Buyer should understand.

#### L-25. SPA Tax-Return Preparation and Review Procedures (Section 7.1) Not Addressed

SPA Section 7.1 requires the Company to prepare and timely file all
pre-closing tax returns (including the final short-period S-corporation
return), to provide Parent a draft at least **30 days** before the due
date, and to give Parent **15 days** to comment, with the parties
cooperating to resolve disputes before filing. The Memo (Section IX.B)
lists the final short-period return as a required filing but does not
describe the SPA's preparation/review timeline or Parent's review
rights. This is relevant to closing coordination, particularly given
that the 2024 return and the final short-period return are both due
around the closing date (L-16).

## IV. Consolidated Findings Table", "old_string": "financing summary should be completed to show all closing sources and
uses.

SPA Section 7.3(b) establishes a contractual procedure for finalizing

IV.C) presents its allocation as "preliminary and subject to adjustment
based upon the final asset valuation to be completed by Thornfield" but
does not mention the SPA's 90/30/30-day finalization procedure or the
independent-firm dispute-resolution mechanism. Given that the Memo and
Thornfield currently disagree by \$76,500,000 in goodwill (C-2, H-3),
this procedure is likely to be invoked; the Memo should describe it.

#### L-22. Shareholder Form 8023 Execution Deadline Is 30 Days Before the Filing Deadline (November 15, 2025), Not December 15, 2025

The Memo (Section IV.A and Appendix A) states that the Form 8023 filing
deadline is December 15, 2025. SPA Section 7.3(c) additionally requires
that "each Shareholder shall execute such form in a timely manner and,
in any event, **no later than thirty (30) days prior to the applicable
filing deadline**" --- i.e., by **November 15, 2025**. The Memo does not
mention this earlier shareholder-execution deadline. Given the
shareholder-consent collection effort required (especially for the
unverified-eligibility holders in C-1), the November 15, 2025 deadline
is the operative date for shareholder action and should be flagged.

#### L-23. Transfer Taxes (SPA Section 7.5) Not Addressed in the State-Tax Analysis

SPA Section 7.5 provides that "All transfer, documentary, sales, use,
stamp, registration, value added, and other similar Taxes and fees ...
incurred in connection with the Merger ... shall be borne and paid fifty
percent (50%) by Parent and fifty percent (50%) by the Shareholders."
The Memo's state-tax analysis (Section VI) does not address transfer or
documentary taxes or the 50/50 allocation. While Delaware imposes no
stock-transfer tax, North Carolina and other states may impose
documentary or transfer taxes on the merger consideration; the Memo
should address this dimension and the contractual allocation.

#### L-24. Beacon Tax-Distribution Obligation and BBA Partnership-Representative / Push-Out Election Not Addressed

The Beacon Operating Agreement (Section 7.02) requires Beacon to
distribute to each member, within 90 days after each fiscal year end, an
amount equal to the member's share of Beacon taxable income multiplied
by the highest combined federal/state marginal rate for North Carolina
residents. Section 11.02 designates Prism as the "Partnership
Representative" under the BBA partnership-audit regime (Section 6223),
with authority to bind the company and members in tax audits, and
Section 11.04 authorizes a Section 6226 push-out election. After the
change of control, new Target (Prism) remains the Partnership
Representative and 80.5% member. The Memo (Section VIII) does not
address (a) Beacon's tax-distribution obligation as it affects the

## V. Recommended Priority Actions

**Before treating the structure as final / before filing Form 8023:**

1.  **Resolve the S-election eligibility question (C-1, M-8).** Obtain
    immigration/residency documentation for Lin Wei Zhang, Yusuf
    Al-Rashidi, and Nina Petrova for every year since their grant dates;
    investigate the alleged family-LLC transfer; confirm eligibility of
    all shareholders for all years since January 1, 2017. If any
    termination occurred, the Section 338(h)(10) election is unavailable
    and the entire structure (including the no-built-in-gains-tax
    conclusion) must be re-evaluated.
2.  **Correct the ADSP and restate the purchase price allocation (C-2,
    H-2, H-3).** Recompute the ADSP from the stock amount realized plus
    target liabilities; reconcile the aggregate adjusted tax basis to
    the per-asset schedule; restate the Class V
    software/developed-technology line; recompute the goodwill residual,
    the deemed sale gain, the per-shareholder allocations (on a
    fully-diluted basis per M-5), and Buyer's amortization schedules.
    Reconcile with Thornfield before Form 8883.
3.  **Deliver the QSBS analysis (C-3).** Prepare the promised Section
    1202 analysis addressing the C-to-S conversion, the 338(h)(10)
    interaction, the \$50M gross-assets and holding-period tests, the
    per-shareholder scope (reconciled to the cap table), and the escrow
    component.

**Before closing / as conditions to closing:**

4.  **Reconcile the escrow tax treatment with SPA Section 7.4 (H-1).**
    Either amend the SPA or withdraw the Memo's deferral advice.
5.  **Address the Beacon issues (H-4, M-1, M-2, M-3).** Confirm whether
    the IP License Agreement will be renewed post-closing; analyze the
    ROFR timing and the tax consequences if exercised; analyze Section
    751 hot assets; evaluate a Section 754 election.
6.  **Resolve the NC data processing license (H-5).** Obtain a
    definitive ruling on whether the license survives the change of
    control.
7.  **Analyze the R&D credits (H-6) and the Section 482 exposure
    (H-7).** Determine whether the \$3.8M credits can be salvaged;
    commission a transfer-pricing study and reflect the \~\$6.3M
    exposure in the indemnity/risk allocation.
8.  **Address the New York sales-tax exposure (M-9)** and complete the
    state-tax analysis (M-11, M-12), including non-resident
    withholding/composite returns and California sourcing.

**Documentation/correction items (Low severity):** Correct the date,
section-citation, regulation-citation, and nomenclature errors (L-1
through L-4, L-10, L-14); reconcile the shareholder count and residency
(M-6), the H-1B nationality (L-5), the Beacon fee CPI history (L-6), the
Thornfield section citations (L-7), Dr. Chandra's basis (L-8), the
rollover destination (L-9), the NC rate (L-11), the conversion
recommendation (L-12), the non-compete recharacterization risk (L-13),
and the financing sources/uses (L-15).

## VI. Limitations

This report is based solely on the seven documents listed in Section II.
No independent verification of the underlying facts was performed;
factual matters (e.g., shareholder eligibility, the existence of the
family-LLC transfer, the survival of the NC license) are stated as they

{"command": "cd $WORKSPACE_DIR && python3 -c "
import re
with open('output/audit_working.md') as f:
    txt = f.read()
# Count finding headers
for sev,prefix in [('Critical','C-'),('High','H-'),('Medium','M-'),('Low','L-')]:
    # Count #### headers
    ids = re.findall(rf'^#### {prefix}(\d+)\.', txt, re.M)

text:
The completeness audit is complete. Here is a summary of what the audit found and what I added.

## Completeness Audit Results

My first draft contained **37 findings**. The mandatory re-read of all seven source documents against the deliverable identified **12 additional distinct issues** the record supports but that I had omitted, plus **3 enhancements** to existing findings. I added all of them.

### 12 new findings added

**2 new Medium:**
- **M-13 — Counsel-role inconsistency.** The Memo and CSW's own email describe CSW as the Buyer's (Ridgeline's) tax counsel, but SPA §2.6(b) pays CSW $2,200,000 "as Company counsel" (Prism's counsel) out of seller transaction expenses — a conflict bearing on the Memo's scope, privilege, and reliability. This was a significant omission.
- **M-14 — Memo's open-items list and conclusions are incomplete.** The Memo's §IX.C lists only 4 routine items and §X reaches 7 favorable conclusions, omitting every critical/high-severity open item the diligence record flags (S-election, QSBS, transfer pricing, R&D credits, NC license, Beacon IP license, Section 751/754, NY sales tax).

**10 new Low:**
- **L-16** — 2024 Form 1120-S due March 15, 2025 (same day as closing), not yet filed, extension likely (Thornfield §IV.A); absent from the Memo's filings list.
- **L-17** — NC R&D credits lack a formal third-party study (Thornfield Finding 6); audit-disallowance risk not flagged.
- **L-18** — Funded debt "assumed by the Buyer" (Memo §IV.B) vs. "repaid in full" (SPA §2.6(a)) — factual inconsistency.
- **L-19** — $58M EBITDA (Memo §VII.B) uncorroborated; ~67% margin on $87M revenue appears high and drives the §163(j) conclusion.
- **L-20** — Tax indemnification structure (SPA §7.6) not addressed; relevant to §482, NY sales-tax, and R&D-credit risk allocation.
- **L-21** — SPA allocation-finalization procedure (90/30/30-day, independent firm, §7.3(b)) not addressed.
- **L-22** — Shareholder Form 8023 execution deadline is 30 days before filing (Nov 15, 2025), not just Dec 15, 2025 (SPA §7.3(c)).
- **L-23** — Transfer taxes 50/50 (SPA §7.5) not addressed in the state-tax analysis.
- **L-24** — Beacon tax-distribution obligation (OA §7.02) and BBA partnership-representative/push-out (OA §11.02/§11.04) not addressed.
- **L-25** — SPA tax-return preparation/review procedures (30-day draft, 15-day comment, §7.1) not addressed.

### 3 enhancements to existing findings
- **C-1** — Added the $2,775,000 aggregate consideration at risk for the three unverified S-corp holders (per the cap table's Consideration Waterfall).
- **H-4** — Added that the Memo's §II.B claim the merger "preserves … contracts" is directly contradicted by the Beacon IP license auto-termination.
- **H-5** — Added that the Memo's §II.B claim the merger "preserves … government licenses" is directly contradicted by the NC license's express non-transferability.

### Verification
- The deliverable now contains **49 distinct findings** (3 Critical, 7 High, 14 Medium, 25 Low), each with its own explicit treatment and source citation.
- The executive summary, consolidated findings table (49 rows), and recommended priority actions were all updated to reflect the new findings.
- All required dimensions confirmed present: executive summary, all 7 source documents referenced, all 4 jurisdictions (NC, CA, VA, NY) covered, all key parties addressed.
- The .docx validates against the ECMA-376 schema (exit code 0).
